27.name: Sri Lanka Aragalaya
27.outcome_summary: The Aragalaya successfully removed the Rajapaksa family from power—a historic achievement. However, the Parliament that replaced Gotabaya with Ranil Wickremesinghe (a Rajapaksa loyalist) implemented harsh IMF-mandated austerity. The new constitution that protesters demanded was never drafted. In 2024, Anura Kumara Dissanayake (NPP/JVP) won the presidency partly on the Aragalaya's legacy—an indirect but significant political shift. The economic crisis that caused the Aragalaya remains only partially resolved.

progress_markers.event: Gotabaya Rajapaksa elected on nationalist platform; implements economic mismanagement including fertilizer ban and tax cuts depleting reserves
COVID-19 destroys tourism (Sri Lanka's major forex earner); foreign reserves collapse from $7.5 billion to near zero
Overnight ban on chemical fertilizers destroys rice harvest; food prices surge; Rajapaksa family ignores expert warnings
March: Pharmacies empty of medicines; fuel queues stretch 3km; 13-hour daily power cuts; foreign debt default announced
March 31: Spontaneous gathering outside Gotabaya's private residence without party organization—'GotaGoHome' emerges
GotaGoGama protest camp outside Presidential Secretariat grows into parallel community with library, medical tent, free food, yoga—cross-ethnic, cross-religious
May 9: PM Mahinda Rajapaksa resigns after pro-government thugs attack protest camp; Wickremesinghe appointed PM
July 9: Hundreds of thousands storm Presidential Palace; Gotabaya Rajapaksa flees to Maldives then Singapore
July 14: Gotabaya Rajapaksa resigns—first president in Sri Lankan history removed by popular protest
July 20: Parliament elects Ranil Wickremesinghe as president—seen as establishment continuity candidate, not reform
July 22: Military raids GotaGoGama, forcibly removes protesters; opposition leaders arrested under PTA (Prevention of Terrorism Act)
IMF $2.9 billion bailout secured under harsh austerity conditions; electricity tariffs tripled; VAT increased; civil service wages cut
Sri Lanka settles external debt with bilateral creditors; economy stabilizes but poverty rates remain high
September: Anura Kumara Dissanayake (AKD/NPP) wins presidential election—first left-wing president in Sri Lankan history; NPP wins parliamentary supermajority in November
AKD government attempts to renegotiate IMF terms and prosecute Rajapaksa-era corruption; accountability for protest crackdown pending

28.name: Thailand Student Democracy Movement
28.outcome_summary: The movement achieved no structural reforms. Constitutional demands were rejected; calls for monarchy reform were ruled unconstitutional by the Constitutional Court. Hundreds of protesters faced lèse-majesté prosecutions. The 2023 elections showed the movement's cultural impact—progressive Move Forward party won the most seats—but was blocked from forming government and its leader subsequently banned. Thailand's democratic regression continued under Prayuth and his successor Srettha Thavisin.

progress_markers.event: Military coup (NCPO) overthrows elected government; Prayuth Chan-o-cha seizes power; political opposition driven underground
Junta manipulates 2019 election through constitutional engineering; Prayuth 'wins'; youth anger at rigged transition intensifies
February: Future Forward Party dissolved by Constitutional Court; student 'flash mobs' erupt at universities across Thailand
March-July: COVID-19 suppresses protests temporarily; economic pain from pandemic deepens grievances
August 10: Student protest at Democracy Monument; first public call for monarchy reform in modern Thai history by Panusaya Sithijirawattanakul ('Rung') reading 10-point manifesto
Three-finger Hunger Games salute adopted across Thai protest movement; Harry Potter and Hamtaro memes used to critique monarchy without explicit lèse-majesté violations
October 14-21: Mass protests; state of emergency declared in Bangkok; water cannons with dye used against protesters at Patumwan intersection
Constitutional Court rules that monarchy reform demands constitute attempt to 'overthrow' the constitutional monarchy—essentially criminalizing the movement's core demand
Mass lèse-majesté charges filed; Arnon Nampa, Parit Chiwarak, Jatupat Boonpattararaksa among hundreds charged; many held in pretrial detention
Hunger strikes by imprisoned activists draw international attention; UN Special Rapporteurs condemn prosecutions
1,634+ charged under various laws (lèse-majesté, sedition, COVID regulations); movement activists face decade-long legal battles
May elections: Move Forward party wins most seats (151/500); shows movement's cultural/electoral impact
Move Forward blocked from forming government by military-appointed Senate; Pheu Thai forms coalition with military parties instead
Move Forward renamed People's Party after Constitutional Court dissolution; leader Pita Limjaroenrat banned from politics for 10 years
Lèse-majesté prosecutions continue; People's Party remains largest opposition; structural reforms remain blocked by constitutional design

29.name: Japan Anti-Nuclear Movement
29.outcome_summary: The movement achieved its immediate goal—all 50 reactors shut down by 2013. But the Abe government reversed course from 2015 onwards, restarting reactors despite sustained public opposition. As of 2024, approximately 12 reactors are operational with more restarts planned. The movement demonstrated that Japan's democratic culture can mobilize mass public dissent for the first time in decades, but could not ultimately override energy security imperatives when economic and geopolitical pressures (post-Ukraine energy crisis) aligned against them.

progress_markers.event: March 11: Magnitude 9.0 earthquake and tsunami triggers Fukushima Daiichi triple meltdown; 150,000 evacuated from 20km exclusion zone
March-June: Government radiation data revealed as systematically understated; independent monitoring shows exclusion zone too small; public betrayal narrative emerges
July: PM Naoto Kan orders stress tests for all nuclear plants; 35 of 54 reactors offline by end of 2011
September 19: 60,000-person anti-nuclear rally at Meiji Shrine complex—first significant political mass protest in Japan in decades
May 5: Last operating reactor (Tomari Unit 3) shut down; Japan briefly achieves 'zero nuclear'
Sayonara Genpatsu petition reaches 7.5 million signatures—largest petition in Japanese history; Nobel laureate Kenzaburo Oe leads protests
July: 200,000 Friday night protests outside PM's residence in Tokyo—largest in decades; cross-generational participation
September: DPJ government announces 'nuclear-free by 2030s' policy; new Nuclear Regulation Authority established
December: Abe LDP wins election; explicitly reverses nuclear phase-out and plans reactor restarts
Abe government includes 20-22% nuclear in energy mix by 2030 in draft energy plan; nuclear restart process begins
August: Sendai reactor restarted—first in four years; nuclear restart program begins under new NRA safety standards
2011 Kumamoto earthquakes reignite safety fears but do not halt restart program; courts occasionally block specific restarts
Russia-Ukraine war causes energy crisis; Kishida government announces acceleration of reactor restarts and potential new plant construction
GX (Green Transformation) policy approved; Japan reverses decades of post-Fukushima direction; long-term operation extensions approved
12+ reactors operational; public opposition remains high (60%+) but overridden by energy security policy; decommissioning of older reactors continues

Major utilities (Kansai Electric, Kyushu Electric) pushing for restarts

30.name: Pakistan Aurat March
30.outcome_summary: The Aurat March has not produced specific legislation but has fundamentally shifted the public discourse around women's rights in Pakistan. The annual march continues despite violent threats and legal challenges. 'Mera jism meri marzi' (My body my choice) became one of the most contested political slogans in Pakistani history. The Domestic Violence Act was passed in Sindh (2013, strengthened) and Punjab (2021) partly in this movement's wake. However, femicide rates remain high, honor killing protections remain weak, and the movement faces intensifying conservative backlash.

progress_markers.event: Women's Action Forum founded in response to Zia ul-Haq's Islamization laws; Pakistan's first organized feminist movement
Malala Yousafzai wins Nobel Peace Prize; global attention on Pakistani women's rights but limited domestic political change
Qandeel Baloch (social media star) murdered in 'honor killing' by brother; murder broadcast on social media creates national shock
Parliament passes Anti-Honor Killing law (Criminal Law Amendment Act)—first explicit criminalization of 'honor' as defense for murder
#MeToo global wave reaches Pakistan; Pakistani women begin speaking publicly about harassment in universities and workplaces
March 8: First Aurat March in Karachi, Lahore, Islamabad—International Women's Day marches explicitly framed as feminist
Second Aurat March; 'Mera jism meri marzi' poster triggers national controversy; religious groups and PTI politicians condemn march; FIRs (police complaints) filed
Backlash paradoxically amplifies movement nationally and internationally; diaspora Pakistanis join globally; march becomes annual fixture
Imran Khan government attempts to frame Aurat March as 'anti-Islamic' and 'Western-funded'; High Court petitions to ban march; courts reject bans
Punjab Domestic Violence Act passed with stronger provisions than previous law
Fifth Aurat March; largest ever; 'Inquilab aurat ka' (Women's revolution) becomes new rallying cry
Multiple court challenges to march permits; violence against some participants; Imran Khan-affiliated religious groups organize counter-marches
Aurat March continues; no major national legislation; discourse shift measurable in media coverage and political language around women's issues

31.name: Myanmar Civil Disobedience Movement
31.outcome_summary: The CDM was crushed in urban areas by sustained military violence. By mid-2021, over 1,000 protesters had been killed and thousands imprisoned. The junta maintained control of major cities. The pro-democracy resistance morphed into an armed struggle (People's Defence Force) that has achieved significant territorial control in ethnic areas by 2024-2025, but at enormous civilian cost. Myanmar is in civil war with no near-term path to democratic resolution. The CDM spirit continues in resistance zones but the non-violent movement was definitively suppressed.

progress_markers.event: Partial democratic opening under Thein Sein government; Aung San Suu Kyi released from house arrest; NLD participates in politics
NLD wins landslide election; Suu Kyi effectively leads government as State Counsellor; military retains 25% of parliamentary seats by constitutional design
November: NLD wins second landslide election; military claims fraud but election monitors find results credible
February 1 (pre-dawn): Military arrests Suu Kyi, President Win Myint, and elected government members; coup announced on state TV
February 2-5: Civil Disobedience Movement (CDM) launches; doctors, nurses, teachers, civil servants refuse to work for junta—largest professional strike in Myanmar's history
Three-finger salute (borrowed from Thai protesters) adopted; daily street protests with hundreds of thousands; international community condemns coup
February 28: Junta uses lethal force; first protesters killed; UN Special Envoy Thomas Andrews reports security forces firing on crowds
March-April: Systematic campaign of arrests, torture, killings; internet shutdowns; crackdowns intensify; protests driven underground in cities
May: People's Defence Force (PDF) formed by CRPH; armed resistance begins; ethnic armed organizations ally with pro-democracy forces
Junta executes four prominent democracy activists including Ko Jimmy and Phyo Zeya Thaw—first executions in Myanmar in decades
Civil war expands; PDF and ethnic armed groups control significant territory in Sagaing, Magway, Chin State, Kayah
Operation 1027: Brotherhood Alliance offensive seizes 200+ military outposts in Shan State; major territorial defeats for junta
Major towns in multiple states fall to resistance forces; junta territory significantly reduced; military under severe pressure
Civil war continues; junta controls Naypyidaw and major cities; NUG (National Unity Government) administers resistance areas; outcome uncertain

32.name: Philippines Anti-EJK / Duterte Accountability Movement
32.outcome_summary: The movement raised significant international awareness but failed to stop the drug war killings while Duterte was in power. The ICC opened a preliminary inquiry and then formal investigation, but Philippines withdrew from the ICC in 2019. Killings slowed after Duterte left office (constitutionally barred from re-election in 2022). Leila de Lima was imprisoned for 6 years and released in 2023. No senior official has been prosecuted domestically. Marcos Jr. continued many Duterte-era policies. Environmental defender killings remain among the world's highest.

progress_markers.event: Philippines already world's most dangerous country for environmental defenders per Global Witness data; 'red-tagging' of activists established practice under successive governments
May: Rodrigo Duterte wins presidential election on explicit extermination rhetoric; 'war on drugs' begins immediately
First months: estimated 3,000-7,000 killed in drug war operations and vigilante killings; police kill alleged drug suspects with near-total impunity
Senator Leila de Lima launches Senate investigation into drug war killings; government targets her in retaliation
February: De Lima arrested and imprisoned on drug charges widely seen as fabricated; placed in detention for 6 years

Maria Ressa wins Nobel Peace Prize—international recognition of Philippines press freedom crisis
ICC Prosecutor Bensouda determines drug war crimes may constitute crimes against humanity; formal investigation authorized
Duterte constitutionally barred from re-election; killings drop significantly under Marcos Jr.; de Lima's legal situation improves
February: Leila de Lima released from detention; conviction overturned by Supreme Court
ICC investigation continues; Duterte arrested in 2025 and surrendered to ICC—historic development; domestic impunity for most killings continues

33.name: Indonesia Anti-Corruption KPK Defense
33.outcome_summary: Students partially succeeded in withdrawing some of the September 2019 legislation package but failed on the most important piece: the KPK revision. The new KPK supervisory board effectively hobbled the body's independence. KPK conviction rates dropped dramatically post-2020. Indonesia's anti-corruption gains from 2003-2019 were significantly reversed. Jokowi's administration continued despite weakened oversight. The movement demonstrated the vulnerability of independent institutions to rapid legislative assault.

progress_markers.event: KPK (Corruption Eradication Commission) established by post-Reformasi legislation; becomes most trusted institution in Indonesia
KPK faces first political attack ('Cicak vs. Buaya' — gecko vs. crocodile) when police attempt to frame KPK leaders; public backlash saves KPK
Jokowi first term: KPK prosecutes high-profile politicians including from PDI-P (Jokowi's own party); tensions grow between KPK and political establishment
September: Parliament moves to revise KPK law during brief recess before new parliament takes office; rushed through without public consultation
September 23-24: Student protests at parliament in Senayan; hundreds of thousands mobilize across 20+ cities; police use tear gas, rubber bullets; 2 students killed
Government withdraws several bills from legislative package under pressure (Criminal Code revisions, Land Bill, Correctional Institution Bill)
KPK revision law passes despite protests; new supervisory board (Dewan Pengawas) installed; KPK employees must re-qualify as civil servants—process removes dozens of experienced investigators
Under new KPK structure: conviction rate drops from 100% to around 96%; investigation pace slows; high-profile politicians increasingly escape scrutiny
KPK employee ethics committee clears almost all candidates in competency tests after firing 56 'compromised' employees—widely seen as gutting institutional capacity
KPK head Firli Bahuri (installed under new governance structure) faces multiple ethics complaints; investigation of minister leads to bribery accusation against Firli himself
Firli Bahuri suspended and named as bribery suspect—shows KPK not entirely dead—but damage to institutional independence is severe
Prabowo Subianto (Jokowi son-in-law's father-in-law) becomes president; KPK reform prospects uncertain; Indonesia's Corruption Perceptions Index has worsened

34.name: Bangladesh Garment Workers / Rana Plaza
34.outcome_summary: Rana Plaza produced the Bangladesh Accord on Fire and Building Safety—the first legally binding international corporate accountability agreement in garment supply chains. Over 200 brands signed. Factory inspections improved measurably. However, minimum wages remained inadequate ($95/month by 2024 vs. $177 demanded by workers). The Accord's original 5-year mandate required repeated renewal campaigns. Sohel Rana was convicted in 2023 after a decade-long trial. Structural power imbalance between brands and workers persists; Bangladesh brands still compete primarily on price, not safety.

35.name: Fridays for Future Global South Expansion
35.outcome_summary: The Global South expansion of FFF produced a measurable shift in the movement's racial and geographic politics—Vanessa Nakate, Mitzi Jonelle Tan, Ayisha Siddiqi, and others became central international voices rather than peripheral ones. The AP photo-cropping incident paradoxically accelerated this change. Loss-and-damage language entered UNFCCC negotiations more forcefully. However, no specific policy wins can be attributed to Global South FFF specifically; the movement remains better at cultural influence than policy conversion.

36.name: Bolivia Cochabamba Water Wars
36.outcome_summary: The Water Wars achieved their immediate demand completely—Bechtel/Aguas del Tunari expelled and water privatization reversed. Oscar Olivera became a global symbol. The movement influenced the UN's 2010 recognition of water as a human right. It catalyzed Bolivia's broader leftward turn, directly enabling Evo Morales's 2005 election. Bolivia enshrined rights of nature in its 2009 constitution. The Cochabamba water system remains publicly managed as of 2024. This is one of the cleanest wins in the dataset.

37.name: Ecuador Rights of Nature / Pacha Mama Constitution
37.outcome_summary: Ecuador created the world's first constitutional rights of nature framework—a genuine breakthrough in legal philosophy. Multiple subsequent court rulings have invoked Rights of Nature (most notably the 2022 Amazon case ruling against oil extraction in Yasuni-ITT, upheld by referendum in 2023). However, the Correa government that passed the constitution simultaneously expanded oil and mining extraction, demonstrating that constitutional rights without enforcement culture are limited. The framework has traveled globally—Bolivia, multiple US cities, New Zealand river personhood—making its normative impact larger than Ecuador's domestic record suggests.

38.name: Mexico Ayotzinapa 43
38.outcome_summary: The Ayotzinapa case produced significant political consequences—it permanently discredited Peña Nieto and contributed to AMLO's 2018 election—but failed at its core demand: finding the 43 students and bringing perpetrators to justice. As of 2025, most students remain unaccounted for. Mexico's 'historical truth' narrative was definitively discredited by the GIEI independent expert group. High-level convictions remain elusive; the Guerrero cartel-state nexus that enabled the disappearances has been partially disrupted but not dismantled.

39.name: Guatemala Anti-Corruption CICIG Protests
39.outcome_summary: The 2015 anti-corruption protests achieved the remarkable goal of forcing a sitting president to resign—unprecedented in Guatemala. CICIG's existence as an international validator with real prosecutorial power was the decisive factor. However, the 2019 expulsion of CICIG under US/Trump pressure reversed institutional gains. Subsequent elections have been contested; Bernardo Arévalo won the 2023 presidential election on anti-corruption platform and survived a coup attempt against his inauguration—showing the movement's lasting effects on political culture even as institutions have weakened.

40.name: Peru Anti-Boluarte Protests
40.outcome_summary: Protests demanding new elections and Boluarte's resignation achieved neither outcome. Boluarte remained in power as of 2025 despite calls for resignation. At least 49 protesters were killed by security forces, with deaths concentrated in indigenous Andean regions. The Defensoría del Pueblo (Ombudsman) documented human rights violations. No accountability for deaths. Castillo faces ongoing corruption trials. The protests successfully demonstrated the depth of Lima-vs-regions structural divide but produced no structural change.

41.name: Honduras Berta Cáceres / COPINH Anti-Dam Movement
41.outcome_summary: The Agua Zarca dam was canceled in November 2016—a clear, specific win. German company Voith Hydro withdrew, followed by Dutch FMO and Finnish FINNFUND. DESA (dam company) executives were convicted for Cáceres's murder in 2021. Berta Cáceres's assassination paradoxically amplified the movement globally, demonstrating that the murder of Goldman Prize winners creates international accountability pressure. However, Honduras remains the world's most dangerous country for environmental defenders, and the root causes—military-corporate alliance, weak rule of law—remain unchanged.

42.name: Haiti PetroCaribe Anti-Corruption Movement
42.outcome_summary: The movement correctly identified the systematic theft of PetroCaribe funds and created massive political pressure, but produced no accountability and no structural change. President Moïse survived both protest waves. He was assassinated in July 2021 in an unrelated plot. Haiti descended into gang-controlled chaos by 2022-2024, with an estimated 80% of Port-au-Prince controlled by gangs. The movement diagnosed the problem accurately but accelerated instability without building institutional alternatives, contributing to state collapse.

43.name: Brazil Amazon Defenders / Yanomami Crisis
43.outcome_summary: Lula's government produced measurable results: Amazon deforestation reduced 50% in 2023 vs. Bolsonaro-era peak; Yanomami humanitarian emergency declared and military deployed; killers of Bruno Pereira and Dom Phillips convicted. However, illegal mining expulsions are incomplete; garimpeiro operations have partially resumed; broader extractive pressures persist. The structural contradiction—Brazil's economy depends partly on Amazon extraction—remains unresolved. The environmental recovery is real but fragile.

44.name: Tunisia Arab Spring
44.outcome_summary: Tunisia achieved the only genuine democratic transition from the Arab Spring—Ben Ali fled, elections were held, a new constitution passed (2014), and the National Dialogue Quartet won the Nobel Peace Prize. However, President Kais Saied conducted a 'soft coup' in July 2021, suspended parliament, and subsequently installed a presidential system through a new constitution (2022) with vastly expanded executive power. Tunisia's democratic experiment lasted roughly 7 years before reverting to soft authoritarianism—making the final score genuinely contested.

July 25: Saied suspends parliament, fires prime minister, seizes emergency powers—widely described as soft coup; some initial public support due to frustration with parliament
New constitution passed by referendum (low turnout); concentrates power in presidency; effectively ends parliamentary system; opposition boycotts
Saied wins re-election (low turnout, opposition arrested); civil society space narrowing; Tunisia's democratic gains substantially reversed

45.name: Egypt Arab Spring [FAILED TIP]
45.outcome_summary: Egypt's Arab Spring failed definitively. Mubarak fell (February 2011), democratic elections were held (2012), but military coup (July 2013) returned Egypt to authoritarianism more severe than Mubarak's. Rabaa Massacre (August 14, 2013) killed 800-1,000 Brotherhood supporters in one day. Sisi's Egypt has more political prisoners, more executions, and more surveillance than Mubarak's. This is the canonical failed-tip case: won the battle (Mubarak gone), lost the war (democracy destroyed).

progress_markers.event: June: Khaled Said beaten to death by police in Alexandria; 'We Are All Khaled Said' Facebook page (admin Wael Ghonim) accumulates 450,000 members
January 25 'Police Day': Day of Rage called following Tunisia's success; Tahrir Square occupation begins
February 11: Mubarak resigns after 18 days of protests; SCAF (military council) takes power
SCAF extends emergency law; attacks on protesters continue; virginity tests on women protesters; 'Parliament' dissolved and reconstituted
June: Mohamed Morsi (Muslim Brotherhood) elected president—first democratic election in Egypt's history
November: Morsi issues constitutional declaration granting himself near-absolute power; secular opposition turns against Brotherhood
June 30: Millions protest Morsi government; military issues 48-hour ultimatum
July 3: General Sisi leads military coup; Morsi arrested; military returns to power
August 14: Rabaa and Nahda Square massacres—security forces clear pro-Morsi sit-ins; 800-1,000 killed; Human Rights Watch calls it 'crime against humanity'
New constitution approved by referendum; Sisi 'elected' president with 96% in low-turnout election; emergency law extended
Giulio Regeni (Italian researcher documenting labor organizing) found tortured to death in Cairo; state of human rights catastrophic
Sisi 'elected' to second term; only opposition candidate allowed had previously expressed support for Sisi; political space near zero
Egypt has 60,000+ political prisoners; journalist Mohamed El-Baqer and Alaa Abd El-Fattah (imprisoned since 2011 revolution); Sisi in third term after constitutional change

46.name: Iran Green Movement [FAILED TIP]
46.outcome_summary: The Green Movement was crushed by September 2009. No electoral reforms resulted. Leaders Mousavi and Karroubi remain under house arrest as of 2025 (14+ years). The movement's networks survived underground and re-emerged in 2019 and 2022 protests. Neda Agha-Soltan's death became a global icon but could not protect the movement from state violence. Iran's political system remained fundamentally unchanged; if anything, IRGC power increased in the wake of the crackdown.

progress_markers.event: Khatami elected on reform platform; reformist decade (1997-2005) builds civil society before Ahmadinejad reversal; 'Reformist infrastructure' survives underground
Ahmadinejad wins election (disputed); reformist political space severely constrained; economic mismanagement accelerates; youth frustration builds
June 12: Official results show Ahmadinejad winning with implausible 63% margin; Mousavi and Karroubi supporters immediately dispute; 'Where is my vote?' protests begin same night
June 15: Largest protest in Iranian history since 1979; 3 million marching in Tehran in silence; eerie, disciplined, powerful
June 20: Neda Agha-Soltan shot dead on Tehran street; filmed by bystander; video transmitted globally within hours—most documented political killing in history to that point
June-September: Basij and IRGC systematically suppresses protests; Kahrizak detention center torture; opposition leaders arrested
February 11 (revolution anniversary): Final attempt at protest crushed; movement effectively over as visible force
February: Mousavi and Karroubi placed under house arrest (to present, 14+ years) after calling for sympathy protests with Arab Spring
November: Fuel price protests (40% overnight increase); regime kills 1,500+ in 4 days—shows the continuity of repressive capacity but also underground resistance
Mahsa Amini protests (Case #47) demonstrate Green Movement's legacy—same networks, same generation's children; movement was suppressed but not destroyed
Mousavi and Karroubi still under house arrest; no accountability for 2009 crackdown; Green Movement's organizational networks survive as underground infrastructure

47.name: Iran Mahsa Amini Protests
47.outcome_summary: The protests were the largest and most sustained challenge to the Islamic Republic since 1979. The slogan 'Woman, Life, Freedom' became global. But the regime survived, and no structural political change resulted. The Guidance Patrol was briefly suspended then reinstated in new form with enhanced technology (cameras, phones). New hijab enforcement legislation (stricter) was passed in 2023. 551+ protesters were killed; 19,000+ arrested; several protesters executed. 'Woman, Life, Freedom' lives as a global frame but failed to change the Iranian state.

progress_markers.event: November: Fuel price protests; regime kills 1,500+ in 96 hours; demonstrates repressive capacity but also depth of societal anger—precondition for 2022
Summer: Hijab protests already escalating; activist Sepideh Rashno arrested for not wearing hijab; widespread solidarity even from non-political women
September 13: Mahsa (Zhina) Amini arrested by Guidance Patrol for 'improper hijab'; September 16: Dies in custody
September 16-20: Protests begin at Kasra Hospital, Amini's family outside; spread to Saqqez (her hometown) and rapidly nationwide
'Jin, Jiyan, Azadî' (Woman, Life, Freedom) in Kurdish becomes movement's global slogan—Kurdish feminist origins of the slogan honored; amplified as universal feminist frame
Female students remove hijabs in schools and universities; high school girls burning headscarves publicly; unprecedented defiance across generations
October-December: Security forces kill 551+ protesters; IRGC and Basij use live ammunition; Baloch and Kurdish protesters disproportionately targeted
Guidance Patrol (morality police) announced 'suspended' by Attorney General November 2022—never officially confirmed, operationally reduced briefly
Four protesters executed by Iran for alleged crimes committed during protests; dozens more on death row; thousands still imprisoned
New Hijab and Chastity law passed June 2023—harsher penalties than before; cars with unveiled women can be impounded; businesses can be closed
Guidance Patrol reinstated under new 'Noor' (Light) plan using CCTV cameras; AI-assisted enforcement; repression continues with technology upgrade
'Woman, Life, Freedom' lives as global feminist frame; networks inside Iran maintain quiet resistance; no structural political change; Khamenei still Supreme Leader

Nazanin Boniadi (actress, became major international spokesperson)
government_champions: No domestic government champions in a theocracy

international: FIFA (Iran played World Cup during protests; team complicated relationship with movement)

48.name: Lebanon Thawra (October Revolution)
48.outcome_summary: Lebanon's October Revolution failed to produce any structural political change. PM Hariri resigned but sectarian system continued. COVID then the Beirut Port Explosion (August 4, 2020—218 killed, 300,000 homeless) further devastated the country. By 2021-2022, Lebanon had the world's highest inflation rate and 90%+ poverty rate. Political class remained unchanged; Hezbollah's political influence continued; no new constitution was drafted. The movement correctly diagnosed Lebanon's terminal kleptocracy and could not cure it.

progress_markers.event: #YouStink garbage crisis movement demonstrates mass mobilization capacity but achieves only limited concessions; establishes civil society activist generation
Economic crisis worsens through 2019; electricity cuts 20 hours/day in some areas; bank dollar withdrawals restricted; government hasn't passed budget in 10 years
October 17: WhatsApp tax announcement ($0.20/day) + government failure to fight Shouf forest fire triggers spontaneous street explosions simultaneously across the country
Human chain across entire country—first time in Lebanese history citizens physically connected from Tripoli to Tyre despite sectarian geography
October 29: PM Hariri resigns—first visible concession from political class
'Kellun ya3ne kellun' (All of them means all of them) becomes movement's demand for complete political class replacement; unprecedented cross-sectarian solidarity
Hezbollah and Amal supporters attack protest camps in Beirut; sectarian factions try to contain or discredit movement
January: Banks continue restricting withdrawals; economic collapse accelerates; COVID lockdowns suppress protests in March-April
August 4: Beirut Port Explosion—2,750 tons of ammonium nitrate stored unsafely for 6 years; 218 killed; 7,000 wounded; 300,000 homeless
Post-explosion rage briefly revives protests but political class survives; Hassan Diab government resigns; no accountability for explosion
Lebanese pound loses 90%+ of value; bank accounts effectively wiped out; poverty rate reaches 78%; World Bank describes as 'deliberate depression'
Judge Tarek Bitar's Beirut blast investigation repeatedly sabotaged; politicians face immunity claims; Lebanon remains in political stalemate
Joseph Aoun elected president January 2025 (after 2-year vacuum)—very small progress; sectarian system essentially unchanged; no accountability for explosion or economic collapse

IMF (negotiations deadlocked due to political obstruction)
World Bank (published devastating assessment of Lebanon's 'deliberate depression')

49.name: Israel Judicial Reform Protests
49.outcome_summary: The protests successfully delayed the judicial overhaul for approximately 9 months—a tactical win. The first reform element (eliminating the 'reasonableness' standard) was passed in July 2023 but struck down by the Supreme Court in January 2024 (13-0 vote). October 7, 2023 Hamas attacks suspended the protest movement as the country went to war. By 2025, the Netanyahu government is pursuing judicial reform through different mechanisms under cover of wartime. The democratic crisis was delayed, not resolved.

progress_markers.event: November: Netanyahu returns to power with most right-wing coalition in Israeli history; Itamar Ben-Gvir and Bezalel Smotrich in cabinet; judicial overhaul announced
January 7: Judicial overhaul package announced; first protests begin; legal experts warn of fundamental damage to democratic checks
January-March: Saturday night protests grow from tens of thousands to 100,000+ weekly; 'brothers and sisters' framing; IDF reserve pilots announce strike
March 26: Netanyahu fires Defense Minister Gallant who warned judicial overhaul threatening military readiness; 400,000 protest Ayalon Highway in spontaneous response
March 27: Netanyahu announces 'pause' of judicial overhaul—first major concession; business sector, defense community pressure was decisive
July: Knesset passes first element of overhaul (eliminating 'reasonableness' standard) despite resumed protests; Netanyahu resumes package
October 7: Hamas attacks; 1,200 killed; 250 hostages taken; country goes to war; protest movement suspended
November: War cabinet formed; emergency coalition dilutes far-right influence temporarily
January: Supreme Court strikes down reasonableness law 13-0; Netanyahu government refuses to comply—constitutional crisis
Judicial reform pursuit continues through other legislative tracks; Netanyahu under ICC warrant; protest movement partially resumes around hostage deal and democratic concerns
Democratic crisis ongoing; government replacing attorney generals and state prosecutors; judicial independence under sustained attack; wartime conditions suppress full protest revival

50.name: Jordan Bread Protests
50.outcome_summary: The protests achieved their immediate demand—PM Hani Mulki resigned and the tax bill was withdrawn for revision. But Jordan's structural economic problems remain: IMF conditions continue, youth unemployment persists, and inequality has not been addressed. The withdrawal was tactical; a modified tax law was subsequently passed. The Jordan protests demonstrate how economic-shock protests can extract concessions from regimes that maintain legitimacy through material distribution, without producing structural reform. The professional unions' cross-class mobilization was a genuine innovation.

progress_markers.event: Arab Spring reaches Jordan; modest protests; King Abdullah announces reform program; some constitutional amendments; protest movement contained through modest concessions
Jordan negotiates 3-year IMF Extended Fund Facility ($723 million); austerity measures begin; fuel and food subsidies reduced; cost of living rising
April-May: IMF pushes income tax law reform as condition for continued support; draft law would double tax rates for middle class; professional unions organize opposition
May 30: General strike by 33 trade unions; June 1: Midnight fuel price increase triggers immediate street protests
June 1-4: Unprecedented scenes—lawyers, doctors, engineers leading protests alongside workers and poor; 'We want to bring down the government' chant in Amman
June 4: PM Hani Mulki resigns; Omar Razzaz appointed; Razzaz announces intention to withdraw income tax bill
June 7: King Abdullah accepts withdrawal of income tax bill for further consultation; protests cease; movement wins tactical demand
Modified income tax law passed with some revisions; IMF structural adjustment continues; underlying conditions unchanged
COVID-19 economic shock compounds austerity; IMF provides additional support with further conditionality; Jordan's debt-to-GDP exceeds 110%
Professional unions continue to be major political voice; teachers' union protests 2022 suppressed; some gains in civil society space
Jordan's economy dependent on Gulf aid and US/IMF support; structural reform stalled; professional unions remain best-organized civil society sector

51.name: Morocco: 2018 Youth Boycott Campaign
51.outcome_summary: Consumer boycott of three politically connected companies (Centrale Danone/milk, Afriquia/petrol, Sidi Ali/water) caused 40-50% revenue drop for Danone Morocco. Prices reduced on some products. Economic impact was real and sustained over months. However, politically the campaign failed: PM Aziz Akhannouch (owner of Afriquia) won the 2021 elections with a landslide, becoming Prime Minister. No structural change to the nexus of political and economic power. A consumer campaign that worked economically but failed politically.

progress_markers.event: Morocco's Hirak Rif movement (#22) builds popular resistance consciousness; young Moroccans increasingly connecting economic grievance to political power
Growing awareness via social media of economic concentration: three major consumer goods companies owned by families with close palace/government ties
Morocco's cost of living rising; fuel prices reaching record highs despite Afriquia owner Aziz Akhannouch serving as Agriculture Minister
April 2018: WhatsApp boycott campaign launched targeting Centrale Danone (milk), Afriquia (petrol), and Sidi Ali (water). Spreads virally within days to millions of participants
By mid-2018: Danone Morocco reports 40-50% revenue decline. Company forced to cut prices and review product lines. Immediate economic impact confirmed
Fuel prices reduced at Afriquia stations. Brief acknowledgment of public pressure from corporate side
Government and palace maintain silence, treat boycott as economic matter, refuse political framing. No political leaders held accountable
Boycott sustained for 5+ months — demonstrating unusual consumer discipline. WhatsApp infrastructure proves effective for coordinating economic action
Danone announces restructuring; eventually partially withdraws from some product segments. Interpreted as corporate defeat but also job losses creating backlash
Movement dissipates without structural political change; organizers face some legal harassment

Aziz Akhannouch (Afriquia owner, direct target of boycott) wins September 2021 elections with massive majority; becomes Prime Minister. Boycott's economic damage did not translate to political accountability

51.what_actually_changed: Danone Morocco's revenues dropped ~40-50% during peak boycott; company restructured and cut some prices. Afriquia and Sidi Ali saw reduced sales. These were real economic impacts. What did NOT change: the political economy linking business elites to political power. Akhannouch became Prime Minister in 2021, demonstrating consumer boycotts cannot substitute for political organizing. The boycott created no lasting institutional change, no regulatory reform, no accountability mechanism. The campaign's model (specific targets, WhatsApp dissemination, precision of three companies) is widely studied as a consumer action template.

52.name: Yellow Vests France
52.outcome_summary: Won significant economic concessions (fuel tax cancelled, €10B in relief package including minimum wage increase) but produced no structural change to inequality, no lasting organization, and may have accelerated right-wing political shift. Macron survived politically. Marine Le Pen and RN strengthened significantly in subsequent elections (2022, 2024). Classic 'battle won, war lost' pattern.

progress_markers.event: Macron elected on 'President of the Rich' narrative; wealth tax abolished ('ISF'); fuel tax increases announced as ecological measure
October 2018: Facebook groups organizing against fuel taxes begin. Priscillia Ludosky's petition reaches 300,000+ signatures. Érık Drouet and other organizers calling for November 17 action
November 17, 2018: 300,000 'Yellow Vests' (fluorescent safety vests — mandatory in all French cars) blockade roundabouts and roads across France. Symbol accessible to everyone
November 24: Violence erupts in Paris; Arc de Triomphe vandalized. First major confrontation between black-bloc elements and mainstream protesters

December 10: Macron addresses nation; announces €10 billion package including minimum wage increase (SMIC +€100/month), no ISF reinstatement but cancellation of CSG increase for pensioners under €2000/month, and fuel tax suspension
Fuel tax fully cancelled January 2019. Specific economic demands largely met within 4 weeks — unusual speed of government response
Grand Débat National launched (nationwide consultation); 10,000 local meetings. Largely absorbed movement energy without structural reform
Movement continued through 2019 but weekly numbers declining. No formal leadership meant no negotiation possible. Far-right elements increasingly visible
European elections (May 2019): Macron's LREM and Le Pen's RN tied at ~23% each. Movement energy partially captured by RN narrative
COVID-19 March 2020 ends street protest phase. Movement infrastructure (leaderless Facebook groups) unable to adapt to pandemic context
Presidential election: Le Pen reaches run-off with Macron again, stronger than 2017. Yellow Vest economic grievances had energized right-wing populism more than left
July 2024 legislative elections: RN achieves plurality in first round; Yellow Vest-originated economic grievances now fully captured by far-right framing

lead_orgs.name: No formal organization
lead_orgs.description: Deliberately leaderless; Facebook groups served as coordination but no national leadership emerged — prevented co-optation but also prevented consolidation

lead_orgs.name: Priscillia Ludosky
lead_orgs.description: Informal leader who launched the original petition; later attempted to channel movement into policy demands

lead_orgs.name: Érık Drouet
lead_orgs.description: Key online organizer; later arrested for protest organization

funders: Self-funded by participants; no major institutional backing
celebrities: No major celebrity involvement; movement explicitly anti-elite

villains: BNP Paribas and major banks (seen as ISF abolition beneficiaries)
Vinci (motorway toll operator — significant roundabout blockades targeted toll plazas)

international: Movement spread to Belgium, Netherlands, Canada; influenced global 'cost-of-living crisis' political vocabulary
Inspired similar movements in multiple countries 2019-2022

52.what_actually_changed: The €10 billion concessions package was real: minimum wage increase, CSG relief for pensioners, overtime tax relief. The fuel tax was permanently cancelled. These were tangible economic wins affecting millions. What did not change: the structural inequality that generated the movement, the wealth tax abolition, or the political economy that Macron represented. Long-term: the movement fed right-wing populism (RN) more than left-wing reform, a pattern repeated globally when economic grievance movements lack organizational infrastructure to channel them into progressive institutional alternatives.

53.name: Occupy Wall Street
53.outcome_summary: No direct policy wins. Zuccotti Park occupation cleared November 2011. But: 'We are the 99%' permanently entered political vocabulary and inequality became a mainstream political issue. Bernie Sanders 2016/2020 campaigns, Elizabeth Warren's Wall Street regulation agenda, and the broader democratic socialist revival all drew directly from Occupy's vocabulary and networks. Cultural impact substantially exceeded operational failure.

progress_markers.event: Global Financial Crisis: banks bailed out ($700B TARP), homeowners foreclosed, unemployment reaches 10%. Anger at Wall Street impunity grows but finds no political outlet
Citizens United ruling (January 2010) confirms corporate capture of democracy. Dodd-Frank Act (July 2010) seen as inadequate bank reform. No bankers prosecuted

Arab Spring and Spanish Indignados provide global permission structure for occupation tactic. Adbusters magazine (Canada) publishes 'What is Our One Demand?' calling for Occupy Wall Street September 17
September 17, 2011: ~1,000 protesters march in Manhattan's financial district; ~100 establish Zuccotti Park occupation. 'We are the 99%' frame coined by anthropologist David Graeber and Adbusters
October 1: 700+ arrested on Brooklyn Bridge; international media coverage explodes. Movement spreads to 900+ cities globally
October 15: Global Day of Action; protests in 1,500+ cities across 82 countries — the first genuinely global synchronized protest in history
November 2: Oakland General Strike — first US general strike in 65 years. Port of Oakland shut down. Movement's tactical peak
November 15: Zuccotti Park cleared by NYPD at 1am; Bloomberg decision coordinated with other mayors (Oakland, Portland, etc.) in nationwide coordinated clearance
May Day 2012 Occupy revival fizzles; movement cannot reconstitute physical presence without occupation tactic
Occupy's vocabulary and networks seed multiple successor projects: Strike Debt, Rolling Jubilee (debt cancellation mutual aid), and local labor campaigns
Seattle $15 minimum wage campaign (Kshama Sawant) draws directly from Occupy network and vocabulary — first major policy win traceable to Occupy
Bernie Sanders 2016 presidential campaign explicitly uses 'the 99%' frame, raises $220M from small donors — demonstrating Occupy's lasting infrastructure contribution
Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) membership explodes from 6,000 to 50,000+ post-2016; directly built on Occupy-era organizer networks

lead_orgs.name: Adbusters Media Foundation
lead_orgs.description: Canadian culture-jamming magazine that issued the original call; Kalle Lasn coined 'We are the 99%' frame

lead_orgs.name: NYC General Assembly
lead_orgs.description: Horizontal decision-making body that governed the Zuccotti occupation; model of direct democracy that proved both inspiring and functionally paralysing

lead_orgs.name: US Day of Rage
lead_orgs.description: Original organizing group; quickly superseded by the occupation itself

funders: Small donations via online fundraising; National Lawyers Guild (legal support)
Working Families Party provided some infrastructure support
celebrities: Naomi Klein, Cornel West, Michael Moore visited and spoke
Russell Simmons, Susan Sarandon publicly supported

villains: JPMorgan Chase (donated $4.6M to NYPD during Occupy)

international: Global Day of Action (Oct 15) connected to Spanish Indignados, Tahrir Square veterans, and movements in 82 countries
Occupy model was adopted in Hong Kong 2014, Brazil, and multiple European countries

53.what_actually_changed: No direct policy wins from the occupation itself. But inequality entered mainstream political discourse in the US for the first time since the New Deal era. The $15 minimum wage movement, Bernie Sanders campaigns, DSA growth, and the broader progressive policy agenda (Medicare for All, Green New Deal, student debt cancellation) all trace organizational roots and vocabulary to Occupy. The most successful 'failed' movement in recent US history — failed as an occupation, succeeded as a cultural and organizational seedbed.

54.name: Polish Women's Strike (Strajk Kobiet)
54.outcome_summary: Direct policy reversal not achieved — the near-total abortion ban (effective Jan 2021) was implemented. But the political consequences were transformative: PiS lost the 2023 elections partly due to the mobilized women's vote (abortion was #1 issue for women under 40 in exit polls). The Tusk coalition government (since 2023) has been attempting to liberalize abortion law, blocked by Presidential veto from Duda (PiS-aligned). Score 4 reflects the major electoral win that is translating into policy, albeit slowly.

progress_markers.event: PiS government proposes total abortion ban ('Black Monday' October 2016); Women's Strike (Strajk Kobiet) organizes massive protests with black clothing as symbol; government backs down on total ban
October 2016 PiS total abortion ban withdrawn under protest pressure — first major defeat of PiS government on social policy
PiS systematically packs Constitutional Tribunal with loyalists; feminist organizations build networks anticipating further attacks on reproductive rights
PiS wins re-election; abortion further restricted in practice through hospital refusals. Black lightning bolt symbol (zaraza) adopted as movement symbol
October 22, 2020: Constitutional Tribunal (packed with PiS nominees) rules fetal abnormality insufficient justification for abortion — near-total ban. Red lightning bolt symbol spreads instantly
Protests disrupt Catholic masses — extraordinary in deeply Catholic Poland. Lightning bolts appear in church windows, on cars, businesses. 400+ cities simultaneously
Government delays implementation until January 2021 under protest pressure, but does not withdraw ruling
January 27, 2021: Ban comes into effect. First woman dies due to denied abortion of non-viable fetus (Izabela from Pszczyna, September 2021) — becomes the movement's martyr figure
October 2021: One year anniversary march; death of Izabela galvanizes second wave of protests. 'To była dziewczynka' (She was a girl) becomes new slogan
Multiple women die from delayed care under near-total ban; each death triggers new protests and media cycles. Medical profession openly criticizing law
October 15, 2023: PiS loses parliamentary elections. Exit polls confirm abortion was #1 issue for women under 40. Women's electoral mobilization decisive in result
Donald Tusk coalition government formed December 2023; includes explicit commitment to liberalize abortion law
Sejm (parliament) passes abortion decriminalization bill but President Duda (PiS-aligned, term until 2025) vetoes. Legal reform stalled but political trajectory clear
Presidential elections scheduled May 2025; pro-choice coalition candidate potentially ending veto block. Movement's electoral victory being slowly converted to policy

lead_orgs.name: Federacja na rzecz Kobiet i Planowania Rodziny (FEDERA)
lead_orgs.description: Policy and legal expertise organization; documented health impacts of the ban

funders: George Soros / Open Society (contested but documented)
celebrities: Kayah (singer), Kortez and other Polish musicians publicly supported
International solidarity from Emma Watson, Billie Jean King and others
government_champions: KO (Civic Coalition) — all in current governing coalition

villains: Ordo Iuris (conservative legal institute, drafted the restrictions)
allies: Some Polish corporations publicly supported women's rights in response to boycott threats

international: European Parliament resolutions condemning Polish abortion restrictions
ECHR (European Court of Human Rights) ruled Poland's abortion restrictions violate human rights (2020, 2023)

54.what_actually_changed: The near-total abortion ban is still in effect as of early 2026, but: (1) Multiple women have died and their cases created ongoing political pressure; (2) PiS lost the 2023 elections with abortion as the decisive issue; (3) The Tusk government has parliamentary majority to change the law, blocked only by presidential veto; (4) Presidential elections in 2025 represent the final institutional barrier. The movement won the long game politically while losing the immediate legal battle — a score of 4 reflects a major win that is still being implemented.

55.name: Iceland: Banking Revolution / Pots and Pans (Búsáhaldabyltingin)
55.outcome_summary: Complete success: government fell, bankers were imprisoned (26 senior executives, highest per capita globally), the country refused to bail out foreign creditors, and the economy recovered faster than comparable crisis countries. Crowdsourced constitution drafted (though not fully adopted). The only country that responded to the 2008 financial crisis through criminal accountability rather than austerity. Score 5 — sustained systemic change achieved.

progress_markers.event: Iceland's three major banks (Kaupthing, Glitnir, Landsbanki) have expanded to borrow 10x GDP. Financial system growing at unsustainable rate; some domestic critics ignored
October 2008: All three banks collapse within a week as interbank lending freezes globally. Iceland's stock market drops 90%. Savings wiped out. Country essentially bankrupt
October 2008: Demonstrations begin; Icesave accounts crisis (UK and Dutch depositors demand Iceland guarantee deposits). Emergency IMF bailout package negotiated
January-February 2009: 'Pots and Pans Revolution' — weekly Saturday protests with pots, pans, drums. PM Haarde becomes first world leader to resign due to financial crisis (January 26, 2009)
April 2009: New left-leaning government under Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir formed. First openly lesbian head of government globally
March 2010: Icesave referendum — Icelanders vote 93% AGAINST government guaranteeing Icesave deposits to foreign creditors. Government accepts the democratic verdict
Special Prosecutor appointed to investigate bank executives; arrests begin
Second Icesave referendum (April 2011): 59% reject again. EFTA Court later rules Iceland was not obligated to bail out depositors — referendum vindicated
Crowdsourced constitution drafted via social media (citizens submitted amendments online). First crowdsourced national constitution. Parliament did not adopt it in full but process was unprecedented
First convictions of bank executives. Eventually 26 senior bankers convicted and imprisoned — sentences of 2-5 years. Highest per capita banker imprisonment globally from GFC
Economy fully recovered — unemployment back near pre-crisis levels. GDP growth positive. Outperformed Ireland, Greece, Portugal which took austerity route
EFTA Court ruling confirms Iceland was right to refuse Icesave guarantee. €6 billion debt avoided. Economic vindication of protest-driven political decisions

lead_orgs.name: InDefence (Indefence.is)
lead_orgs.description: Civil society organization that coordinated early demonstrations and Icesave referendum campaign

lead_orgs.name: Hagsmunasamtök Heimilanna
lead_orgs.description: Association of household savings defenders; organized debt relief campaign

lead_orgs.name: Iðunn (feminist organization)
lead_orgs.description: Part of the broad coalition; women's organizations central to Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir government coalition

funders: Self-organized through civil society; no major external funders
celebrities: Björk publicly supported the protest movement internationally
international: UK and Dutch governments (demanded Icesave guarantee — became the foreign pressure that domestic democracy successfully resisted)

55.what_actually_changed: Iceland is the only country in the world that prosecuted and imprisoned senior bankers for their role in the 2008 financial crisis. The economy recovered fully by 2013 without imposing austerity (unlike Ireland, Greece, Portugal). Icesave debt avoided entirely. A new constitution was drafted (though not fully adopted). The pots and pans revolution produced the most comprehensive accountability outcome of any GFC-related movement globally — a complete success within Iceland's context, limited exportability to larger economies.

56.name: Spain Indignados / 15-M Movement
56.outcome_summary: No immediate policy wins. Movement cleared from plazas by autumn 2011. But long-term institutional impact was profound: Podemos party (founded 2014 from 15-M networks) entered government in 2020 as junior coalition partner. Municipal activists ('Mayors of Change') won city halls in Madrid, Barcelona, and other major cities in 2015. The two-party system (PP/PSOE duopoly) was permanently disrupted. Score 3 reflects cultural/institutional shift that took years to convert into policy.

progress_markers.event: Spain's real estate bubble collapses; unemployment begins rising to 20%+ (youth unemployment 50%+). Mortgage crisis creates massive evictions. Partido Popular and PSOE seen as identical on economic austerity
General strike November 2010 against socialist government's austerity measures. Traditional union-led protest — precursor to 15-M's more horizontal approach

¡Democracia Real Ya! ('Real Democracy Now') network formed by informal groups. Manifesto circulated online. Inspired by Tunisian revolution. Planned protest for May 15 (day before municipal elections)
May 15, 2011: 130 cities; ~50,000 march. Madrid's Puerta del Sol occupied after police disperse small post-march group. Spontaneous; next morning 20,000 arrive to find 40 people had stayed overnight
May 27: Catalan government attempts to evict Sol occupation; 10,000 surround to prevent eviction. Cross-city solidarity demonstrating resilience
June 2011: Movement transforms from static occupation to 'popular assemblies' in neighborhoods across Spain — institutionalization attempt
Municipal elections (May 22, before movement peak) — PP wins majority of city halls despite protests. Movement unable to convert into immediate electoral impact
Occupations gradually wind down through summer-autumn 2011. No formal leadership = no negotiation possible = no policy wins
15-M networks transform into Platform for Mortgage Victims (PAH) — led by Ada Colau; direct action against evictions becomes Spain's most popular social movement. Stops 1,600+ evictions
January 2014: Podemos launched by academics led by Pablo Iglesias — explicitly channeling 15-M energy into electoral vehicle. Uses circle (assembly) imagery and 15-M vocabulary
Municipal elections May 2015: 'Mayors of Change' (15-M activists) win Madrid (Manuela Carmena), Barcelona (Ada Colau), and several other major cities
December 2015 elections: Podemos wins 20.7% — Spain's political duopoly permanently broken. No government can form without including new parties
January 2020: Pedro Sánchez forms coalition government with Podemos. Pablo Iglesias becomes Deputy Prime Minister. Direct line from Puerta del Sol 2011 to government participation 2020
Podemos splits (2023); Yolanda Díaz's Sumar replaces it in government. 15-M's institutional legacy continues through successor formations

lead_orgs.name: ¡Democracia Real Ya!
lead_orgs.description: Original organizing network; launched the May 15 call. Loose network of individuals without formal structure

lead_orgs.name: Juventud Sin Futuro (Youth Without Future)
lead_orgs.description: Student network that provided early organizational core

lead_orgs.name: Platform for Mortgage Victims (PAH)
lead_orgs.description: Most durable organizational product of 15-M; led by Ada Colau; direct action against bank evictions

funders: Some labor union support (CNT, smaller unions)
celebrities: Pablo Iglesias (political scientist, future Podemos leader, future Deputy PM)

villains: Major Spanish banks (Santander, BBVA, Bankia) — mortgage eviction targets
La Caixa (major savings bank involved in foreclosures)

international: Occupied Wall Street, Occupy London, and global Occupy movements inspired by and connected to 15-M
Podemos model studied by Syriza (Greece), Five Star Movement (Italy), La France Insoumise

56.what_actually_changed: No immediate policy changes from the occupation. But 15-M permanently disrupted Spain's two-party system: Podemos and Ciudadanos (responding to same dynamics from center-right) ended the PP/PSOE duopoly. The PAH (Platform for Mortgage Victims) stopped thousands of evictions and won a municipal mortgage relief law. The 'Mayors of Change' (Colau in Barcelona, Carmena in Madrid) implemented progressive urban policies 2015-2019. Pablo Iglesias as Deputy PM in the 2020-2023 government implemented Spain's first guaranteed minimum income (IMV). The 9-year arc from Puerta del Sol to government participation represents one of the most successful movement-to-institution conversions in recent European political history.

57.name: Urgenda Climate Victory — Netherlands
57.outcome_summary: Historic legal victory: Dutch Supreme Court (December 2019) confirmed government must cut emissions 25% by 2020 — binding legal order. First time a court ordered a government to act on climate based on human rights. Dutch government implemented policy changes to comply. International replication across 30+ countries created the global climate litigation movement. Score 4 (not 5) because compliance has been incomplete and subsequent Dutch governments have partly backtracked.

progress_markers.event: Urgenda Foundation (founded 2008) begins developing the legal theory: could Dutch civil liability law force the government to cut emissions at the rate science requires?
Lawsuit filed against Dutch state by Urgenda Foundation on behalf of 900 Dutch citizens. Novel legal theory: duty of care under Dutch civil law + ECHR human rights
June 24, 2015: District Court of The Hague rules in Urgenda's favor. Government must reduce greenhouse gas emissions at least 25% by end of 2020 (compared to 1990 levels). Historic first
International replication begins immediately: climate lawyers in Belgium, Ireland, UK, Australia, Colombia, Pakistan begin studying the Urgenda template
October 9, 2018: Hague Court of Appeal upholds the lower court ruling. Government's appeal fails. 25% by 2020 confirmed
December 20, 2019: Dutch Supreme Court upholds ruling in landmark decision — first national supreme court to order climate action on human rights grounds. Ruling is final and binding
Dutch government implements emergency policy measures to meet the 2020 target: coal plants partly curtailed, additional renewable policies accelerated
Netherlands reaches approximately 25% reduction target by 2020 — the legal order was formally met, though critics note partial compliance

Neubauer v. Germany (German Constitutional Court ruling April 2021) directly inspired by Urgenda. Torres Strait Islanders (Australia), Sharma v. Minister (Australia), and dozens more cases follow the template
By 2022: 30+ countries have Urgenda-style climate litigation. Roughly 2,000 climate lawsuits globally — Urgenda as the founding case
Dutch farming/nitrogen crisis and political upheaval (BBB party, Wilders rise) creates political backlash against environmental regulation; new Dutch government (2024) weakening climate ambition
Geert Wilders' PVV leads new Dutch coalition government (2024); announces rollback of climate and nitrogen reduction policies. Urgenda legal win being eroded by political shift

lead_orgs.name: Urgenda Foundation
lead_orgs.description: NGO founded by Marjan Minnesma; designed and ran the 8-year litigation strategy

lead_orgs.name: Milieudefensie (Friends of the Earth Netherlands)
lead_orgs.description: Complementary organization; ran parallel Shell liability case (won in 2021)

lead_orgs.name: Climate Litigation Accelerator (later Sabin Center for Climate Change Law)
lead_orgs.description: Amplified the Urgenda template internationally to 30+ countries

funders: Novo Nordisk Foundation and other philanthropy for replication
government_champions: International climate lawyers who adopted the template globally

villains: Shell (separate Milieudefensie case won in 2021 — Shell ordered to cut emissions 45% by 2030)

international: UN Human Rights Committee (confirmed human rights-climate link)
ECHR (used as legal basis in the ruling)
UN Special Rapporteur on Human Rights and Environment (supported legal theory)

57.what_actually_changed: The Urgenda ruling established: (1) Governments can be legally ordered to take climate action under human rights law; (2) IPCC science can be used as a legal standard; (3) Dutch civil law duty of care applies to government climate inaction. Netherlands approximately met the 2020 25% target. More important globally: 2,000+ climate lawsuits now filed in 65+ countries using the Urgenda template. The Shell ruling (2021, separate case) ordered a major oil company to cut emissions by 45% by 2030 — the most significant corporate climate liability ruling ever. Urgenda's lasting contribution is the legal framework, not just the Dutch policy win.

58.name: Germany Constitutional Climate Ruling (Neubauer v. Germany)
58.outcome_summary: Federal Constitutional Court ruled April 2021 that Germany's Climate Protection Act was partially unconstitutional: insufficient targets shifted the burden of sharp cuts unfairly onto future generations. Germany revised its Climate Protection Act within weeks, adopting 'net zero by 2045' target. Rights-of-future-generations framework established in German jurisprudence and spreading globally. Score 4 because implementation is ongoing and the incoming CDU/CSU-led government (2025) is weakening targets.

progress_markers.event: Fridays for Future peaks globally; German government passes Climate Protection Act (November 2019) — widely criticized as insufficient by scientists. NDCs inadequate for 1.5°C pathway
Constitutional complaints filed by: Deutsche Umwelthilfe + BUND (German environmental federation), Greenpeace Germany, and individual FFF activists including Luisa Neubauer (lead petitioner)
Complementary cases filed in Netherlands (Milieudefensie v Shell), France (L'Affaire du Siècle), and UK — building international pressure environment

April 29, 2021: Federal Constitutional Court (Bundesverfassungsgericht) rules 2019 Climate Protection Act partially unconstitutional. Insufficient emissions reduction targets after 2030 violate fundamental rights of younger generations
June 2021: Bundestag passes amended Climate Protection Act within 6 weeks of ruling. Net zero by 2045 target (5 years earlier than previous 2050 target). Binding annual sector targets established
Ruling explicitly uses 'rights of future generations' framework — first major constitutional court to do so for climate. Influences constitutional interpretation globally
Neubauer ruling cited in subsequent cases: Montana (US, Held v. Montana 2023), New Zealand, Brazil, and others adopt rights-of-future-generations framing
German government (Scholz coalition) sets interim targets and begins renewables acceleration. But Russian invasion of Ukraine (Feb 2022) triggers gas infrastructure backslide
Constitutional Court strikes down climate fund (November 2023) — ruling against off-budget climate spending ends €60B Klima- und Transformationsfonds
Scholz coalition collapses; snap elections (February 2025). CDU/CSU wins; new government announced rollback of some climate measures
Net zero 2045 target formally retained in new coalition agreement but implementation timelines stretched; renewables investment commitments reduced

lead_orgs.name: Deutsche Umwelthilfe (German Environmental Aid)
lead_orgs.description: Lead petitioner in the constitutional complaint; environmental NGO known for aggressive legal strategy

lead_orgs.name: BUND (Friends of the Earth Germany)
lead_orgs.description: Co-petitioner; major environmental federation with legal capacity

lead_orgs.name: Greenpeace Germany
lead_orgs.description: Filed separate but parallel complaint; provided media amplification

funders: Small donor networks from FFF community
celebrities: Luisa Neubauer (lead named petitioner; Germany's most prominent FFF activist)
government_champions: Robert Habeck (Green Party, Economy and Climate Minister 2021-2025) — key implementation champion
international: Urgenda Foundation (provided legal template and strategic advice)
IPCC (scientific standard used in legal argument)

58.what_actually_changed: German Climate Protection Act amended within 6 weeks (rare speed for major legislation). Net zero 2045 target is legally binding. Annual sectoral budgets (industry, transport, buildings, agriculture) are legally enforceable. The rights-of-future-generations framework is now part of German constitutional jurisprudence — the most durable contribution. Implementation under the post-2025 CDU-led government is being slowed, but the 2045 target has not been formally abandoned. The international replication (30+ cases citing Neubauer's rights-of-future-generations framework) represents the ruling's most significant lasting impact.

59.name: Anti-Austerity Greece (SYRIZA/Oxi)
59.outcome_summary: Complete failure of democratic expression to override economic coercion. Despite 61.3% 'No' vote in the July 2015 referendum, Greece accepted a third bailout more austere than what voters rejected. SYRIZA governed as austerity implementer for the next four years. The movement demonstrated the limits of national democratic sovereignty within the eurozone architecture — economic coercion (threat of bank closures, euro exit) overrode democratic mandate. Score 1: Awareness only — the movement surfaced the democratic deficit of eurozone governance but produced no structural change.

progress_markers.event: Greece's first bailout (May 2010): €110B + sweeping austerity memorandum. Cuts to pensions, public sector wages, healthcare. GDP begins multi-year contraction
Syntagma Square occupation begins; Indignants (Aganaktismenoi) movement. 100,000+ gathered regularly. 'Real democracy now' framing
Second bailout (July 2011): €130B + deeper austerity. Youth unemployment reaches 50%+. SYRIZA coalition begins growing electorally from 4.6% to 16.8%
Third bailout negotiations; austerity deepens. SYRIZA emerges as main opposition party. Greece's GDP contracted 25% — equivalent to Great Depression scale
SYRIZA leads polls; global Keynesian economists (Stiglitz, Piketty, Varoufakis) build public case that austerity is counterproductive
January 25, 2015: SYRIZA wins elections on explicit anti-austerity platform. Alexis Tsipras becomes PM. Yanis Varoufakis becomes Finance Minister. 'Oxi' (No) to austerity the mandate
Five months of intense negotiations with Troika (ECB, EU, IMF). Varoufakis develops alternative economic proposals. International solidarity from Podemos, Corbyn, Sanders
June 27, 2015: Tsipras calls surprise referendum on whether Greece should accept creditor terms. ECB immediately cuts emergency liquidity to Greek banks — capital controls imposed
July 5, 2015: Greeks vote 61.3% 'Oxi' (No) to creditor demands. One of the largest democratic anti-austerity mandates in European history
July 13, 2015: Despite referendum result, Tsipras accepts third bailout — harsher than what voters rejected. Varoufakis resigns. Germany/Schäuble's economic coercion prevails over Greek democracy
September 2015: Tsipras wins elections again — now as austerity implementer. SYRIZA's capitulation legitimizes the memorandum from within the left
July 2019: New Democracy (conservative) wins elections. SYRIZA loses power having governed as managers of austerity. The anti-austerity movement produced no structural change to eurozone governance
Greek debt restructured; some austerity requirements relaxed as EU recognizes counterproductiveness. But this came from EU political evolution, not Greek protest pressure

lead_orgs.name: SYRIZA (Coalition of the Radical Left)
lead_orgs.description: Electoral vehicle for anti-austerity movement; grew from 4.6% to 36% 2009-2015

lead_orgs.name: ADEDY and GSEE (public and private sector unions)
lead_orgs.description: Organized general strikes; provided organizational infrastructure

lead_orgs.name: Popular Unity (splinter from SYRIZA)
lead_orgs.description: Formed after SYRIZA capitulation; party of those who refused to accept the memorandum

celebrities: Yanis Varoufakis (celebrity economist; international media profile)
Ken Loach, Naomi Klein, international left solidarity

villains: Goldman Sachs (arranged the original debt concealment that enabled Greek deficit to be hidden)
German export industry (major beneficiary of euro architecture that hurt Greece)

international: IMF (disputed with EU over austerity depth; later admitted austerity was excessive)
Wolfgang Schäuble (German Finance Minister; architect of coercion strategy)
ECB (used emergency liquidity as weapon — unprecedented use of monetary policy as political coercion)

59.what_actually_changed: Almost nothing structurally. The Oxi referendum result was ignored. Austerity continued until 2018 (when bailout formally ended). Greek GDP shrank 25% and has only partially recovered. Public debt (the original problem) actually increased as % of GDP during the bailout period — demonstrating austerity's macroeconomic counterproductiveness. What DID change: the democratic deficit of eurozone governance became globally visible; Varoufakis founded DiEM25 (Democracy in Europe Movement) attempting to build a European democratic political project; the analytical case for Keynesian alternatives was legitimized globally.

60.name: Extinction Rebellion — UK Launch
60.outcome_summary: UK Parliament declared 'climate emergency' (largely symbolic). Net Zero by 2050 target legislated (but less ambitious than demanded). No Citizens' Assembly on climate. XR's direct civil disobedience model produced extensive media coverage but limited policy conversion. Post-2020, XR internally fractured (Just Stop Oil splinter); UK government designated XR as 'extremist' organization (2023). Score 2: Partial win — contributed to policy vocabulary shift and net zero legislation but demands not met.

progress_markers.event: Roger Hallam and Gail Bradbrook develop XR from Momentum organizing infrastructure; academic research on civil disobedience effectiveness informs tactics. Extinction Symbol (hourglass) designed
October 31, 2018: 'Declaration of Rebellion' outside UK Parliament. Simultaneous with IPCC 1.5°C report — perfect narrative timing
November 2018: Five Thames bridges (Westminster, Waterloo, Lambeth, Blackfriars, Millennium) blockaded simultaneously — first major action

April 2019: International Rebellion — London shut down for 11 days. Waterloo Bridge occupation creates 'garden protest.' Oxford Circus blockaded. 1,000+ arrests. Simultaneously: 33 countries, 80 cities
May 1, 2019: UK Parliament declares 'climate emergency' — first national parliament globally. Largely symbolic but politically significant
June 2019: UK legislates Net Zero by 2050 target — first major economy to do so. XR's April disruption directly preceded this (though causality contested)
October 2019 London rebellion — police use section 14 Public Order Act to ban protests. Courts later rule ban unlawful, but chilling effect registered
COVID-19 ends street protest model. XR unable to adapt digital strategy effectively. Internal tension over whether non-violence was sufficient tactic
August 2021: 'Impossible Rebellion' in London — smaller scale, more confrontational (targeting banks, newspapers). Public sympathy declining

Just Stop Oil (JSO) splinter forms, taking more radical tactics (slow marching, soup on van Gogh). XR's 'radical flank' effect disputed — JSO arguably reduces public support for broader climate action
UK Counter-Terrorism Police puts XR on 'extremist' monitoring list (later retracted under pressure). Police, Crime, Sentencing and Courts Act (2022) severely restricts protest rights
UK Rishi Sunak government delays EV mandate, weakens boiler heat pump targets, issues new North Sea oil licenses. Net Zero 2050 target retained but implementation timeline stretched
XR continues but at significantly reduced scale. Climate litigation (Client Earth, Urgenda-model) increasingly effective alternative to direct action in UK context

lead_orgs.name: Extinction Rebellion UK

lead_orgs.name: Just Stop Oil

lead_orgs.name: Rising Up!
