funders: Joschka Tulloch and other individual donors
Climate Emergency Fund (US-based funder of disruptive climate activism)
government_champions: Caroline Lucas (Green MP, most consistent parliamentary ally)

villains: News UK/News International (targeted by XR for climate denial coverage)
Barclays and HSBC (targeted for fossil fuel financing)

international: International Civil Disobedience research (Harvard, Gene Sharp's work)

60.what_actually_changed: UK net zero 2050 target legislated (the most durable outcome). UK Parliament climate emergency declaration. But: no Citizens' Assembly. No 2025 net-zero requirement. No 2030 emissions target ambitious enough for 1.5°C. XR's most specific policy demand (Citizens' Assembly) was never met. The Public Order Act (2022) restricts protest rights in ways that directly responded to XR tactics — the movement changed the law against itself as much as for its goals. The direct action model contributed to the Net Zero legislation but was insufficient for the transformation demanded.

61.name: Montreal Protocol — Ozone Layer
61.outcome_summary: The most successful environmental treaty in history. CFCs phased out by all 197 signatory parties. Ozone layer measurably recovering — projected fully healed by 2065 (southern hemisphere) to 2045 (northern hemisphere). The only case of humanity successfully reversing a planetary-scale environmental crisis. Score 5 — full policy change, globally sustained, with measurable physical improvement.

progress_markers.event: Rowland and Molina publish CFC ozone-depletion theory in Nature. DuPont and aerosol industry immediately attack the science — same playbook as tobacco industry
National Academy of Sciences confirms Rowland-Molina theory. US ban on CFC aerosols proposed
US, Canada, and Sweden ban CFC aerosols in consumer products. Industry lobbying prevents global action
NASA satellite data confirms significant Antarctic ozone depletion. Industry continues to dispute
Farman, Gardiner, and Shanklin publish 'ozone hole' discovery in Nature (May 1985). Simultaneous: Vienna Convention for Protection of the Ozone Layer signed — framework without specific targets

'Ozone hole' visual — literally a hole visible from satellite over Antarctica — becomes one of the most powerful environmental communication breakthroughs in history. Connected to skin cancer personally
DuPont business calculation shifts: the company has developed CFC alternatives (HFCs) and begins recognizing the market opportunity in phasing out CFCs it controls
September 16, 1987: Montreal Protocol signed by 46 nations. CFC production to be halved by 1998. 'Ozone hole' having made the harm personally understandable to mass audiences
London Amendment: Phase-out of CFCs accelerated and extended; Multilateral Fund created to help developing countries transition. Full CFC phase-out by 2000 for developed countries
Copenhagen Amendment: Phase-out accelerated further; HCFCs (transitional substitutes) also scheduled for phase-out. Treaty demonstrating ability to self-strengthen
Developed countries complete CFC phase-out. DuPont and other companies profit from selling CFC alternatives — industry transformation complete
Beijing Amendment: Controls extended to new substances. Universal ratification achieved — 197 parties, making Montreal Protocol the only UN treaty with universal participation
Scientific evidence: ozone layer showing measurable recovery in northern hemisphere. The first planet-scale environmental repair underway
Kigali Amendment: HFCs (greenhouse gases used as CFC replacements) also scheduled for phase-down — inadvertently turning ozone treaty into climate treaty
UN report: ozone layer on track for full recovery by 2066 (Antarctic), 2045 (Arctic), 2040 (rest of world). Planet-scale physical proof of treaty effectiveness

lead_orgs.name: UNEP (United Nations Environment Programme)
lead_orgs.description: Secretariat host; Mostafa Tolba's leadership as UNEP Executive Director was decisive in pushing the protocol

lead_orgs.name: Natural Resources Defense Council (NRDC)
lead_orgs.description: Key US environmental NGO that pushed for domestic action and international negotiations

lead_orgs.name: Environmental Defense Fund
lead_orgs.description: Built the economic case for CFC alternatives being profitable

funders: US EPA (key funder of ozone science)
Multilateral Fund under Montreal Protocol ($3.3B for developing country transition)
government_champions: Lee Thomas (EPA Administrator 1985-89, key US negotiator)
Mostafa Tolba (UNEP Executive Director, the most important individual in achieving the treaty)
Margaret Thatcher (unexpectedly became champion — first major leader to frame ozone as global crisis)

villains: DuPont (1974-1986 — funded industry attacks on Rowland-Molina science)
allies: DuPont (1986-1987 — switched to champion once it held CFC alternatives patents)
Reckitt Benckiser and major aerosol companies (switched after US ban)

international: Multilateral Fund (technology transfer to Global South)

61.what_actually_changed: CFC production reduced from 1.1 million tonnes/year (1987) to near zero. Equivalent of 135 billion tonnes of CO2 avoided (climate co-benefit larger than Paris Agreement projected impact in same period). Ozone layer recovering on schedule. Skin cancer rates from ozone depletion averted — estimated 2 million skin cancer cases prevented annually. The treaty's key success factors: (1) visible, visceral harm with personal resonance (skin cancer); (2) alternatives already available; (3) industry calculation shifted; (4) small number of key producing countries; (5) Mostafa Tolba's extraordinary personal diplomatic skill.

62.name: Ottawa Treaty — Anti-Personnel Landmines
62.outcome_summary: Ottawa Treaty (Mine Ban Treaty) signed December 1997; 164 state parties as of 2024. Anti-personnel landmine production and use by signatories effectively eliminated. Annual civilian casualties dropped from ~26,000/year (1997) to ~5,720/year (2022). But: US, Russia, China, India, Pakistan not signatories. Active use continues in non-signatory conflicts (Russia-Ukraine, Myanmar). Score 4 — major policy win, partially implemented (significant majority but not universal).

progress_markers.event: International Campaign to Ban Landmines (ICBL) founded by Jody Williams and six NGOs. Novel strategy: bypass traditional UN disarmament forums; work directly with willing states
ICBL coalition grows to 350+ organizations in 40 countries. Extensive documentation of civilian casualties, especially children and farmers
Belgium becomes first country to unilaterally ban anti-personnel landmines. 'Ottawa process' concept emerges — Canadian government offers to host conference
October 1996: Ottawa Strategy Conference — 50 countries declare intent to ban. Canada announces will host treaty signing conference in 1997. US excluded from 'fast track' process
January 1997: Princess Diana visits Angola minefield — photos of Diana among landmine victims become global front pages. Unprecedented celebrity-cause moment
January 1997: Jody Williams coordinates 1,000+ NGOs via email — one of the first major international movements coordinated primarily by email
August 31, 1997: Princess Diana dies in Paris. Her landmine advocacy had just been filmed in Bosnia. Legacy becomes inseparable from the cause. Treaty signing four months later
December 3-4, 1997: Ottawa Treaty signed by 122 nations. Jody Williams and ICBL awarded Nobel Peace Prize
March 1, 1999: Treaty enters into force — record speed for major disarmament treaty (fastest entry into force of any disarmament treaty ever at that time)
International mine clearance underway. Mine Risk Education programs established globally. Mine Action enters development mainstream
Signatories required to destroy stockpiles within 4 years. Major producers (UK, France, Germany, etc.) complete destruction. 48 million stockpiled mines destroyed by signatories
Libya uses anti-personnel mines during civil war. First signatory to use mines after ratification. Enforcement mechanisms weak
Russia uses anti-personnel mines extensively in Ukraine. Russia was never a signatory. Demonstrates treaty's limitation without universal membership

164 state parties (as of 2024). Annual casualties: ~5,720 (2022) vs. 26,000 (1997). Mine-affected land cleared: 9+ million mines cleared in cleared states. Goal: mine-free world by 2025 (not achieved; extended)

lead_orgs.name: International Campaign to Ban Landmines (ICBL)
lead_orgs.description: 1997 Nobel Peace Prize laureate. Founded by Jody Williams; coordinated 1,000+ NGOs in 60 countries via email

lead_orgs.name: Handicap International (now Humanity & Inclusion)
lead_orgs.description: Key NGO documenting civilian casualties and conducting survivor assistance

lead_orgs.name: Human Rights Watch Arms Division
lead_orgs.description: Documentation, advocacy, and monitoring of treaty compliance

funders: Diana, Princess of Wales Memorial Fund (post-1997)
celebrities: Princess Diana (visits to Angola, January 1997, and Bosnia — defining campaign images)
Paul McCartney (later ambassador for landmine victim support)
government_champions: Lloyd Axworthy (Canadian Foreign Minister — architect of Ottawa process)
Robin Cook (UK Foreign Secretary 1997 — reversed UK position)

villains: Anti-personnel landmine manufacturers (primarily state-owned; US, Russian, Chinese companies major producers)
Mil-Tec (UK private trader — prosecuted for trading)

international: ICRC (International Committee of the Red Cross — key advocacy partner)
GICHD (Geneva International Centre for Humanitarian Demining)
Non-signatories: US, Russia, China, India, Pakistan, Israel

62.what_actually_changed: Production by signatory states effectively ended. 164 countries have never used them since ratification. 53 million stockpiled mines destroyed. Civilian casualties dropped from ~26,000/year to ~5,720/year (2022). Significant mine clearance in former conflict zones (Cambodia, Angola, Bosnia). The norm against their use is now overwhelming — even non-signatories are politically constrained. The US stopped producing in 1997 despite not signing. Major limitation: Russia, China, India, US non-participation means 60%+ of global population not covered. Ukraine conflict (2022-) shows the reality of non-universal treaties.

63.name: Paris Agreement
63.outcome_summary: Historic diplomatic achievement: 196 parties signed; first universal climate agreement. But: NDCs are not legally binding; aggregate pledges still put world on 2.7-3°C trajectory (far from 1.5°C goal); US withdrew under Trump (2017-2021) and is withdrawing again under Trump (2025); enforcement mechanisms essentially absent. Score 3 — cultural/institutional shift without binding policy. The agreement exists; it is not working at the required speed.

progress_markers.event: Copenhagen Climate Summit (COP15) fails catastrophically. Developed vs. developing country impasse. Obama's hallway diplomacy produces non-binding 'accord.' Trust in UNFCCC process collapses
Post-Copenhagen: UNFCCC develops 'bottom-up' nationally determined contributions (NDCs) model — each country sets its own targets. Circumvents the top-down caps deadlock
Durban Platform: countries agree to negotiate a new agreement applicable to all parties (unlike Kyoto, which only bound developed countries) by 2015
Typhoon Haiyan (November 2013): Yeb Saño's (Philippines) tearful speech at Warsaw COP creates global moral pressure moment. Saño fasts for 2 weeks during negotiations
November 2014: US-China bilateral climate deal — removes the 'why should we if they don't?' deadlock that had blocked global agreement for 20 years
September 2014: People's Climate March — 400,000 in NYC, 2,500+ events globally. Largest climate march in history to that date. Political signal ahead of COP negotiations
December 12, 2015: Paris Agreement adopted by 196 parties at COP21. 'Well below 2°C, pursuing efforts toward 1.5°C' goal. Laurent Fabius gavels adoption
Red Lines action: thousands lie in Paris streets holding red cloth creating aerial red-line image — one of the most successful visual activist moments at a treaty negotiation
November 4, 2016: Paris Agreement enters into force (fastest major UN treaty to enter into force)
June 1, 2017: Trump announces US withdrawal from Paris Agreement — 'America First' rationale
Despite US withdrawal: subnational commitments maintained (US Climate Alliance — 25 states). Most NDCs updated. Green financing accelerating
February 2021: Biden administration rejoins Paris Agreement on day one
COP26 Glasgow: NDCs updated but still aggregate to 2.4-2.7°C. Loss and damage discussed but not funded. 1.5°C alive but barely
COP27 Sharm el-Sheikh: Loss and Damage Fund established — first time wealthy nations acknowledge financial responsibility for climate harm. Considered Paris Agreement's most significant advance
January 2025: Trump withdraws US from Paris Agreement again. US also withdraws from international climate finance commitments. Second US withdrawal in 8 years

lead_orgs.name: UNFCCC Secretariat (Christiana Figueres)
lead_orgs.description: Figueres as Executive Secretary 2010-2016 transformed the institutional culture from failure (Copenhagen) to success (Paris). Personal leadership matter enormously

lead_orgs.name: High Ambition Coalition (EU + vulnerable states)
lead_orgs.description: 80+ countries (EU, Marshall Islands, et al.) who pushed for 1.5°C ambition and legally binding elements

lead_orgs.name: Small Island Developing States (SIDS/AOSIS)
lead_orgs.description: Moral authority coalition that made 1.5°C a non-negotiable demand ('1.5 to stay alive')

government_champions: Laurent Fabius (French Foreign Minister — COP21 President)

villains: American Petroleum Institute — lobbied for non-binding provisions
allies: Majority of S&P 500 companies backed Paris Agreement

international: UN Security Council resolution (2014 recognizing climate as security threat)

63.what_actually_changed: The Paris Agreement changed the global norm: climate action is now a universal expectation, not a developed-country obligation. NDC ratchet mechanism provides review pressure. Green finance has accelerated enormously (renewable energy investment >$350B/year). Loss and Damage Fund (COP27) is the first structural recognition of climate reparations. What has NOT changed: actual emissions. Global CO2 emissions have continued rising since Paris. The agreement is the diplomatic achievement of the century — a treaty with 196 parties — that is not working fast enough to achieve its stated goal.

64.name: Plastics Treaty Negotiations
64.outcome_summary: INC-5 (Busan, November-December 2024) collapsed without treaty. Petrostate bloc (Saudi Arabia, Russia, Iran, US) blocked production caps. Petrochemical industry lobbyists outnumbered environmental NGOs 4:1 at negotiations. INC-6 tentatively planned but without US under Trump administration, prospects are severely weakened. Score 1: Awareness only — the campaign succeeded in putting plastics treaty on global agenda but has not achieved binding agreement.

progress_markers.event: Break Free From Plastic coalition formed. Sea turtle straw video (2015) had demonstrated mass public concern. Scientific evidence of microplastics in ocean, seafood, blood, fetal tissue accumulating
EU Single-Use Plastics Directive proposed. Ocean plastic becomes mainstream political issue. David Attenborough's Blue Planet II (2017) reaches 100M+ viewers
March 2022: UNEA-5 resolution in Nairobi — 175 countries vote to start plastics treaty negotiations. Landmark: first time production caps even discussed at UNEP level
INC-1 (Punta del Este, Uruguay, November 2022): First negotiations. High Ambition Coalition forms (EU + 70+ countries pushing for production caps). Industry shows up in force
INC-2 (Paris, May 2023): Technical discussions. Petrostate bloc (Saudi Arabia, Russia, Iran) begin systematically blocking production cap language. Industry lobbying documented
INC-3 (Nairobi, November 2023): Countries remain deeply divided. 'Like-minded group' of oil-producing states demands treaty only address waste management, not production
INC-4 (Ottawa, April 2024): Progress on text but fundamental disagreement on production caps unresolved. US signals ambivalence under domestic political pressure

INC-5 (Busan, November-December 2024): Negotiations collapse without agreement. Petrostate bloc used procedural tactics to block binding production caps. Industry lobbyist outnumber NGOs 4:1 in delegation count
January 2025: Trump administration signals pullback from international environmental agreements. US role in plastics treaty negotiations thrown into question
High Ambition Coalition (EU + 70+ countries) maintains commitment. INC-6 planning underway but timeline, location, and prospect of achieving agreement uncertain

lead_orgs.name: High Ambition Coalition (HAC)
lead_orgs.description: 70+ countries pushing for binding production caps; EU leadership with Marshall Islands, UK, Canada

lead_orgs.name: Break Free From Plastic
lead_orgs.description: 1,900+ NGO coalition that provides civil society pressure; annual Brand Audit names corporate plastic polluters

lead_orgs.name: IUCN (International Union for Conservation of Nature)
lead_orgs.description: Scientific credibility for treaty negotiations; microplastics harm documentation

funders: Minderoo Foundation (Australia — major plastics treaty funder)
government_champions: EU (Frans Timmermans, then Wopke Hoekstra, climate commissioner)
Rwanda (African champion; domestic plastic ban model)

allies: Unilever, Nestlé, Danone (support treaty in principle to avoid stricter national regulations)

international: Scientific consensus from 500+ scientists calling for production caps

64.what_actually_changed: No treaty. Plastic production continues on trajectory to triple by 2050. However: the UNEA-5 resolution did establish the treaty mandate (not nothing); national plastic bans have accelerated globally (70+ countries with some form of ban); EU Single-Use Plastics Directive in force; Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) legislation expanding in many countries. The treaty campaign's most important contribution is keeping production caps on the negotiating table — preventing the industry's preferred outcome of a 'waste management only' treaty.

65.name: COVAX — Vaccine Equity
65.outcome_summary: COVAX failed to meet its 2021 target of 2 billion doses to 92 GAVI-eligible countries. Delivered 1.9 billion doses — less than half the 2021 target. Vaccine nationalism prevailed. IP waiver battle at WTO produced minimal practical result. Omicron variant emerged in under-vaccinated South Africa, demonstrating strategic idiocy of vaccine inequity. Score 1: Awareness only — the campaign surfaced vaccine equity as an issue but produced no structural change to pharmaceutical monopoly power.

progress_markers.event: April 2020: ACT-Accelerator established by WHO/Gavi/CEPI/World Bank. COVAX Facility announced June 2020 as mechanism for equitable vaccine distribution
May 2020: WHO Resolution on IP sharing for COVID tools. US (under Trump) refuses to join COVAX; EU joins. Unprecedented pharmaceutical mobilization
Rich countries (US, UK, EU, Canada) pre-order 3-4x their populations in vaccine doses via bilateral deals — 'vaccine nationalism' establishes fait accompli
February 2021: COVAX delivers first vaccines but faces manufacturing shortfalls. Serum Institute of India (largest COVAX supplier) blocks exports due to domestic Delta wave (April 2021)
May 2021: US announces support for IP waiver at WTO (surprise announcement). Raises hopes for TRIPS waiver on COVID vaccines
EU blocks comprehensive IP waiver at WTO. Pharma lobbying (BioNTech/Pfizer, Moderna) prevents meaningful transfer of mRNA technology
Mid-2021: Africa vaccination rate remains below 3% while rich countries offering booster shots. People's Vaccine Alliance, MSF, Oxfam document and publicize the inequity
November 2021: Omicron variant emerges in South Africa — widely under-vaccinated due to COVAX failures. 'Vaccine apartheid' produces the very outcome experts warned of: new variants
June 2022: WTO agrees limited IP waiver — for COVID vaccines only, for 5 years, for developing countries. Considered inadequate by public health advocates; no mRNA technology transfer
COVAX eventually delivers 1.9 billion doses (vs. 2 billion target for 2021 alone). Africa Centres for Disease Control works on continent-wide vaccine manufacturing (Aspen Pharmacare, etc.)
COVID-19 vaccination: Africa at ~30% fully vaccinated (vs. 70%+ in wealthy countries). mRNA Technology Transfer Hub in South Africa receives limited industry cooperation
WHO Treaty on Pandemic Prevention, Preparedness and Response (PPR) negotiations ongoing — explicitly addresses COVAX failure; still not agreed as of 2025

lead_orgs.name: People's Vaccine Alliance
lead_orgs.description: Amnesty International, Oxfam, MSF and 100+ NGOs demanding IP waiver and COVAX reform

lead_orgs.name: MSF (Médecins Sans Frontières / Doctors Without Borders)
lead_orgs.description: Leading voice on IP access; 'Access Campaign' applied AIDS-era lessons to COVID

lead_orgs.name: Gavi/WHO/CEPI (COVAX operational co-leads)
lead_orgs.description: Multilateral bodies responsible for COVAX; partly captured by pharmaceutical interests in their governance structures

funders: Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation ($1.75B to COVAX)
US government ($4B), EU ($1.2B to COVAX — though bilateral deals undercut COVAX)
government_champions: Cyril Ramaphosa (South Africa — led African IP waiver push)
Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala (WTO DG — pushed for equity)
Biden administration (US IP waiver support, May 2021)

villains: Moderna (refused technology transfer despite NIH funding)
AstraZeneca (relatively better on equity but still protected IP)

international: AU (African Union — established own vaccine manufacturing goals)

65.what_actually_changed: The COVAX failure established the terms of the next debate: the WHO Pandemic Treaty (still being negotiated in 2025) explicitly addresses technology transfer and IP flexibility. The mRNA Technology Transfer Hub in South Africa is a direct COVAX failure response. Africa CDC's ambition for 60% African vaccine manufacturing by 2040 is a direct response. Structural pharmaceutical monopoly power over global health — the core problem — was not changed. The Omicron lesson (vaccine inequity produces new variants that endanger rich countries) was absorbed intellectually but not institutionally.

66.name: Arab Spring — Syria
66.outcome_summary: Catastrophic failure. 500,000+ killed. 13+ million displaced. Chemical weapons used multiple times. Assad survived until December 2024 when he fled (HTS/rebel forces took Damascus). But the post-Assad transition is fraught; HTS is a former al-Qaeda affiliate. No accountability for chemical weapons use. Syria is the most catastrophic outcome in the movement dataset — a peaceful democracy demand ignited a proxy war that destroyed the country.

progress_markers.event: January-February 2011: Tunisia and Egypt provide permission window. Syrian activists, inspired by Arab Spring, begin planning demonstrations. Bashar al-Assad initially dismissive — 'Syria is different'
March 2011: Teenage boys (ages 10-15) in Daraa spray anti-Assad graffiti. Arrested; tortured. Parents' protests met with live ammunition. First deaths. Protests spread nationally
April-June 2011: Massive Friday protests across Syria. Security forces kill hundreds. Defections begin from Syrian army. 'Army of Free Syria' forms June 2011
Assad's Maher al-Assad (brother, 4th Division commander) leads brutal crackdowns in Daraa, Hama, Homs. Russia and China veto UN Security Council resolutions
Movement transforms from protest to civil war. Houla massacre (May 2012, 108 civilians including 49 children). UN Supervisory Mission suspended. International community paralyzed
August 21, 2013: Ghouta chemical attack near Damascus — 1,400+ civilians killed with sarin. Obama's 'red line' crossed; no military response. International credibility collapses
ISIS (ISIL) declares caliphate; conflict multiplies. US, Turkey, Jordan, Gulf states intervene against ISIS. Russia, Iran, Hezbollah intervene for Assad. Syria becomes proxy battlefield
Battle of Aleppo: Assad-Russia-Iran forces retake city with massive civilian casualties. 400,000 besieged; barrel bombing of hospitals documented. Movement's urban strongholds fall
Khan Shaykhun chemical attack (April 2017). Trump orders 59 Tomahawk missiles on Syrian airbase — one-time response, no strategic change
Douma chemical attack (April 2018). Assad consolidates control over most of Syria. HTS controls Idlib. Turkish-backed forces in north
13 million Syrians displaced (6.7M externally, 6.7M internally) — second largest displacement crisis globally. Reconstruction blocked by Western sanctions on Assad

December 2024: HTS-led offensive captures Aleppo (November 28), then Damascus (December 8). Assad flees to Russia. 50-year Assad family dictatorship ends. But post-Assad transition deeply uncertain — HTS's governance record in Idlib mixed

Post-Assad Syria: new transitional government trying to consolidate; ISIS still active in desert; Turkish, Israeli, US military still present; reconstruction requires billions with no clear funding source

lead_orgs.name: Local Coordination Committees (LCCs)
lead_orgs.description: Original civilian protest networks; maintained peaceful framing early in the movement

lead_orgs.name: Free Syrian Army (FSA)
lead_orgs.description: Initial armed resistance; fragmented into dozens of factions

lead_orgs.name: Syrian National Council / Syrian Opposition Coalition
lead_orgs.description: Political exile organizations; never achieved unified leadership or territorial control

funders: Gulf states (Qatar, Saudi Arabia, UAE — funded various armed factions)
international: Russia and China (UNSC vetoes — 16 times on Syria)
ICC (no referral for Syria; Russia/China vetoed)

66.what_actually_changed: The immediate goal (removal of Assad) was achieved in December 2024 — 13 years after the uprising began. But at catastrophic cost: 500,000+ dead, 13M+ displaced, country in ruins. No accountability for chemical weapons. No reconstruction plan. The original movement's civilian democratic leaders were largely killed, imprisoned, or exiled — the post-Assad Syria is being shaped by armed factions, not the 2011 democracy advocates. Syria represents the failure mode when a movement ignites armed conflict in a geopolitically contested space with a sponsor state (Russia) willing to use unlimited force.

67.name: West Papua Independence Movement
67.outcome_summary: One of the world's most persistent suppressed independence movements. No self-determination achieved. Armed resistance (OPM/TPNPB) continues but militarily weak. Indonesian military operations ongoing. Media access to Papua restricted — creating systematic invisibility. International community (Australia, US, EU) unwilling to antagonize Indonesia. Score 0: Suppressed.

progress_markers.event: Indonesia administers West Papua (Dutch New Guinea) following UN resolution. Papuan independence activists banned from organizing
'Act of Free Choice': 1,025 selected Papuan representatives (not general election) vote for integration with Indonesia. UN General Assembly 'takes note' — widely seen as fraudulent. Resistance begins
Freeport-McMoRan copper/gold mine begins operations in Puncak Jaya — one of world's largest. Revenue sharing with local communities minimal; environmental damage significant
Freeport Mine pipeline explosion — blamed on OPM (Free Papua Movement). Major military response; many civilian casualties. Pattern of collective punishment established
Major Papuan cross-border flight to Papua New Guinea following military operations. UNHCR establishes refugee camps
Suharto falls; Reformasi era opens political space briefly. Papuan People's Congress formed; Papua National Congress 2000 attracts 5,000+ delegates
Papua People's Congress (June 2000) raises independence flag, restores name 'Papua' — briefly before military crackdown. President Wahid briefly open to dialogue
Special Autonomy Law passed — more resources to Papua but no self-determination. Theys Eluay (Papuan independence leader) assassinated by Kopassus (Indonesian special forces)
Papuan Spring: massive peaceful demonstrations 2010-2013 for self-determination. Police use live ammunition; dozens killed. International media barely covers due to access restrictions
August 2019: Racist slurs against Papuan students in Surabaya spark massive protests in Papua and across Indonesia. 'Papua is not a monkey' becomes central slogan
Internet shutdown in Papua (6 months — longest in Indonesian history). Military operations in Nduga district displace 40,000+. UN Special Rapporteurs denied access
Lucus Enembe (Papua Governor, independence-sympathetic) arrested. Dozens of activists imprisoned under treason laws. New military command ('Kogabwilhan III') established in Papua
New Zealand news footage of Indonesian military abuses viral; Indonesia withdraws NZ military training cooperation briefly. No structural change. TPNPB kidnaps New Zealand pilot
TPNPB holds NZ pilot hostage 19+ months (record duration); killed February 2025. Military operations ongoing. Papua province divided into 6 provinces — 'divide and rule' strategy

lead_orgs.name: United Liberation Movement for West Papua (ULMWP)
lead_orgs.description: Umbrella political organization; Benny Wenda as international spokesperson

lead_orgs.name: TPNPB (West Papua National Liberation Army)
lead_orgs.description: Armed wing; limited military capacity but maintains visibility through targeted actions

lead_orgs.name: KNPB (National Committee for West Papua)
lead_orgs.description: Nonviolent political organization; members routinely arrested

funders: Diaspora Papuan communities (primarily in Australia, Netherlands, Pacific Islands)
No state sponsors of independence movement
celebrities: Archbishop Desmond Tutu expressed solidarity before his death
government_champions: Pacific Island Forum (regional organization; has called for UN Human Rights monitoring)
Vanuatu (most consistent state voice for West Papua at UN)

villains: Freeport-McMoRan (Grasberg mine — one of world's largest gold/copper mines; ongoing environmental damage)

international: UN Human Rights Council (procedures used; Indonesia rarely cooperates)

67.what_actually_changed: Nothing fundamental. Indonesia remains in control. Mining continues. Military operations continue. Self-determination rights remain denied. The main achievement is international awareness among human rights communities and Pacific Island governments. Australia, the most important potential advocate, consistently prioritizes economic and security relationships with Indonesia over West Papua. The movement continues to exist after 55+ years — demonstrating extraordinary resilience — but no political progress toward self-determination.

68.name: HIV/AIDS: ACT UP Treatment Access
68.outcome_summary: Complete success. FDA drug approval process accelerated from 10+ years to ~6 months. Combination antiretroviral therapy approved 1996; HIV became a manageable chronic condition. AZT price reduced through activism. 'Silence = Death' visual became among the most effective activist communication designs in history. ACT UP's scientific literacy model (patient-experts challenging FDA trial design) was revolutionary. Score 5 — full sustained policy change.

progress_markers.event: First CDC reports of AIDS (June 1981). By 1982, 1,000+ cases. Reagan White House never publicly mentions AIDS. Press Secretary Larry Speakes jokes about it at press conferences
GMHC (Gay Men's Health Crisis) founded by Larry Kramer and others — first AIDS service organization. Crisis continues without government response
12,000+ dead. Larry Kramer publishes '1,112 and Counting' in New York Native — explosive polemic demanding action. Expelled from GMHC for 'too confrontational' stance
Rock Hudson dies of AIDS — first major celebrity death; briefly shifts media coverage. Reagan finally mentions AIDS publicly 1985 (after 20,000+ US deaths)

March 10, 1987: Larry Kramer speech at Lesbian and Gay Community Services Center, NYC — 'Two-thirds of you could be dead within five years.' ACT UP (AIDS Coalition to Unleash Power) founded March 12, 1987
'Silence = Death' project (Gran Fury collective): pink triangle on black background — the most recognized AIDS symbol. Designed before ACT UP but immediately adopted as its visual identity
April 25, 1987: ACT UP's first demonstration on Wall Street — 17 arrested. Demands: FDA accelerate drug approvals. Burroughs Wellcome targeted for AZT pricing ($10,000/year)

October 11, 1988: FDA Headquarters (Rockville, MD) stormed by 1,000 ACT UP members. Die-in outside building. 176 arrested. Protesters hold 'FDA: Murderers' signs. FDA accelerates parallel track approval
September 14, 1989: ACT UP shuts down New York Stock Exchange for 4 minutes — first-ever market disruption by activists. Target: Burroughs Wellcome (AZT maker). Stock drops. AZT price cut 20%
AIDS Treatment Registry established — ACT UP members learn to read FDA trial protocols, identify flaws, and propose alternatives. Patient-expert model revolutionizes drug trial advocacy
Ryan White CARE Act signed — first major federal AIDS funding. The ashes of people who died of AIDS scattered on White House lawn (November 1, 1992)
ACT UP pressure achieves 'parallel track' for AIDS drugs — can be accessed outside formal trials by those who can't meet trial criteria. FDA accelerated approval pathway created
FDA approval process for AIDS drugs accelerated to under 2 years (was 10+ years). ACT UP's scientific technical knowledge becomes indispensable to FDA process
Combination antiretroviral therapy (HAART) approved. Annual AIDS deaths in US fall from 50,000 (1995) to 18,000 (1997) — 64% drop. HIV becomes manageable chronic condition
AZT price reduced from $10,000/year to $3,000/year through sustained campaign pressure and generic competition
Treatment Action Campaign (South Africa — Case #69) wins pharmaceutical patent battle for generic ARVs — direct organizational descendant of ACT UP

lead_orgs.name: ACT UP (AIDS Coalition to Unleash Power)
lead_orgs.description: Founded 1987 NYC; peak 10,000 members. Pioneered 'scientific activist' model — members became pharmaceutical trial experts

lead_orgs.name: Gran Fury (ACT UP art collective)
lead_orgs.description: Designed 'Silence = Death,' 'Read My Lips,' and other campaign visuals — the most effective activist art collective in American history

lead_orgs.name: TAG (Treatment Action Group)
lead_orgs.description: 1992 spinoff from ACT UP — focused on FDA trial design reform; achieved most durable policy changes

funders: Small donations from members and community
celebrities: Elizabeth Taylor (early and consistent celebrity advocate)
government_champions: Rep. Henry Waxman (D-CA) — only consistent Congressional voice demanding AIDS response 1983-1990
David Kessler (FDA Commissioner 1990-97) — implemented ACT UP-demanded reforms

villains: Hoffman-La Roche, Bristol-Myers Squibb (parallel pricing targets)
allies: National Institutes of Health (eventually, after ACT UP forced engagement)

international: ACT UP chapters in 70+ cities globally
AIDS activist networks in UK, Australia, France
Treatment Action Campaign (South Africa) — direct descendant

68.what_actually_changed: FDA drug approval process transformed: 'accelerated approval' pathway created (still exists) specifically from ACT UP pressure. 'Parallel track' for terminal illness access created. Patient advocates required on FDA advisory panels. HIV treatment outcomes: from universal death sentence (1987) to manageable chronic condition (1996 onward). 'Silence = Death' established visual activism as a serious discipline. ACT UP's scientific literacy model (sick people becoming pharmaceutical experts) became the template for patient advocacy globally. Annual US AIDS deaths: 50,000 (1995) → <7,000 (2023).

69.name: South Africa: Treatment Action Campaign / AIDS Treatment
69.outcome_summary: Complete success. 39 pharmaceutical companies dropped lawsuit; South Africa began world's largest ARV treatment program (2004). Zackie Achmat's personal sacrifice (refusing ARVs until all could access them) created unimpeachable moral authority. Doha Declaration (2001) established the global framework for pharmaceutical patents vs. public health — still the foundational legal instrument for access-to-medicines debates globally. Score 5 — full policy change, sustained, with global impact.

progress_markers.event: Post-apartheid South Africa faces HIV epidemic (already 12% prevalence in 1994). Mandela government does not prioritize AIDS — the beginning of a multi-year failure

Treatment Action Campaign (TAC) founded by Zackie Achmat (December 1998). TAC strategy: make HIV-positive people visibly political actors, not passive victims. 'HIV Positive' T-shirts as radical visibility
39 pharmaceutical companies sue South African government over Medicines Act allowing generic ARV production. Mbeki's AIDS denialism begins emerging

Zackie Achmat publicly announces he is HIV-positive and refuses to take ARVs until they are available to all South Africans. Achmat's personal sacrifice creates a moral clarity no advocacy campaign could manufacture
13th AIDS Conference in Durban (July 2000): TAC and global AIDS activists descend on Durban. 12,000+ delegates. ACT UP graduates present. Mbeki defends denialism at opening — globally condemned
April 2001: 39 pharmaceutical companies drop the lawsuit against South Africa after global public pressure becomes untenable. Iconic victory — corporations suing poor country over dying patients

November 2001: Doha Declaration on TRIPS and Public Health — WTO declares that intellectual property rules cannot prevent countries from protecting public health. TAC's campaign was central to creating the political climate for this
Constitutional Court rules government must provide nevirapine to prevent mother-to-child HIV transmission — first major judicial victory for TAC (Minister of Health v. Treatment Action Campaign)
August 2003: South African government finally commits to ARV rollout after sustained TAC pressure, court rulings, and political isolation of Mbeki's denialism
ARV rollout begins April 2004. Within 5 years becomes world's largest ARV program — 5+ million people on treatment
Zackie Achmat finally takes ARVs — treatment is available. His personal commitment kept. 7 years of personal sacrifice completed
Thabo Mbeki removed as ANC president (2007); AIDS denialism ends officially. Kgalema Motlanthe and then Jacob Zuma expand ARV program
South Africa's ARV program: 1 million+ people on treatment. Annual AIDS deaths declining. The movement's central goal achieved
6+ million South Africans on ARVs (world's largest program). HIV incidence declining. Doha Declaration still the global framework for generic medicine access debates 23 years later

lead_orgs.name: Treatment Action Campaign (TAC)
lead_orgs.description: Founded by Zackie Achmat. Peak 60,000+ members, predominantly Black women. Combined legal, street, and international pressure strategies

lead_orgs.name: Medicins Sans Frontieres (MSF) / Doctors Without Borders
lead_orgs.description: Key international partner; ran 'Campaign for Access to Essential Medicines' in parallel

lead_orgs.name: Oxfam International
lead_orgs.description: Trade policy expertise; central to Doha Declaration negotiations

celebrities: Nelson Mandela (publicly took ARVs in 2005 after his son died of AIDS — decisive cultural moment)
government_champions: Justice Edwin Cameron (openly HIV-positive judge; global advocate)
Barbara Hogan (post-Mbeki Health Minister; reversed denialism)

villains: Merck — the 39 companies that sued South Africa
allies: Cipla (Indian generic manufacturer — offered generic ARVs at $1/day; broke the pricing model)

international: WHO (3 by 5 initiative — ARVs for 3M in developing world by 2005)

69.what_actually_changed: ARV prices globally reduced 99% (from $10,000/year to <$100/year for generics) between 2000 and 2010, primarily through the Doha Declaration framework TAC helped create. South Africa's ARV program (6M+ on treatment) is the world's largest. Constitutional Court ruling on nevirapine established that South Africans have a right to healthcare, not just an interest in it. The Doha Declaration is still cited in every access-to-medicines debate globally. PEPFAR ($15B+ US program, bipartisan) was partly a political response to the shaming of the US position in the pharmaceutical lawsuit.

70.name: Jubilee 2000 — Debt Cancellation
70.outcome_summary: Significant debt relief achieved ($100B in G7 Cologne Initiative + HIPC Enhanced Initiative for 41 countries), but not the full cancellation demanded. The 24 million signature petition was the largest in history at that time. Bono's political access was transformative. However, Jubilee South (Global South-led movement) argued the debt relief design was insufficient and unjust. Score 3 — major cultural shift and policy progress, but structural debt architecture unchanged.

progress_markers.event: Growing academic and NGO literature on 'odious debt' — debt incurred by dictators, not owed by their populations. Jubilee concept (biblical 50-year debt cancellation) developed by Martin Dent
Jubilee 2000 campaign formally launched in UK. Church networks (Catholic, Anglican, Methodist, Baptist) provide organizational base. Trade unions, development NGOs join

Campaign begins building around 'Drop the Debt' slogan for G8 Birmingham (1998). 24 million signature petition campaign begins. Biblical 'Jubilee' frame gives moral authority transcending secular politics
May 1998: G8 Birmingham Summit. 70,000 people form human chain around the conference center — 'rings' the summit. Unprecedented civil society pressure at G8
G8 Birmingham: Cologne Debt Initiative announced — $100B in debt relief for 41 countries. First major G8 concession to international development civil society pressure
Bono (U2) and Muhammad Ali publicly champion Jubilee 2000. Bono meets every G8 leader personally — unprecedented celebrity political access achieved
G8 Cologne: Enhanced HIPC Initiative — broader relief for more countries. World Bank and IMF create Fast Track for debt relief for poorest countries
24 million signatures presented to G8 Okinawa summit. Millennium Development Goals adopted (September 2000) — Jubilee framing directly influenced goal #1 (poverty) and debt components
HIPC completion point: 22 countries reach full debt relief eligibility. But process slow, conditions attached (IMF-required economic reforms)
G8 Gleneagles (2005): Multilateral Debt Relief Initiative (MDRI) — complete debt cancellation for 18 countries (all debt to World Bank, IMF, African Development Bank). Bono's continued engagement key
'Make Poverty History' (2005) coalition — direct descendant of Jubilee 2000. White wristbands, Live 8 concert. Bono, Geldof, and celebrity advocacy model reaching its institutional peak
New debt crisis emerging in Global South — Jubilee 2000's relief was insufficient; new lending (China, private markets) replacing cancelled debt

Global sovereign debt crisis: 60% of low-income countries in or near debt distress. Zambia, Sri Lanka, Ethiopia, Ghana defaults. Jubilee 2000's structural critique (debt architecture itself unjust) validated but unsolved

lead_orgs.name: Jubilee 2000 Coalition (UK)
lead_orgs.description: Launched by Ann Pettifor and others; church networks, trade unions, development NGOs

lead_orgs.name: Jubilee South
lead_orgs.description: Global South-led movement that argued Northern-designed debt relief was insufficient; included Jubilee Africa, Jubilee USA, Jubilee Latin America

lead_orgs.name: DATA (Debt, AIDS, Trade, Africa)
lead_orgs.description: Bono and Jamie Driscoll's organization, founded 2002 as successor to Jubilee lobbying

celebrities: Bono (U2) — the most significant single celebrity in international development campaign history
Muhammad Ali (iconic image at Birmingham G8)
Bob Geldof (Live Aid lineage; Make Poverty History)
government_champions: Gordon Brown (UK Chancellor, key architect of HIPC/MDRI)

villains: Vulture funds (bought developing country debt at discount and sued for full repayment — specifically targeted by post-Jubilee advocacy)
Private creditors who resisted debt relief deals
allies: World Bank and IMF (eventually became partners in HIPC process)

70.what_actually_changed: $100B+ in debt relief for 41 countries through HIPC/MDRI by 2015. 35 countries reached completion point, receiving full debt cancellation on eligible debts to World Bank, IMF, African Development Bank. The G8's Cologne and Gleneagles commitments were real (unlike many G8 promises). The 'Drop the Debt' frame permanently changed international development language. However: new debt accumulated rapidly; structural power imbalances in sovereign debt architecture unchanged; as of 2022-2024, Global South debt crisis as bad as 1990s in many dimensions. The victories were real but insufficient.

71.name: Fossil Fuel Divestment Movement
71.outcome_summary: $40+ trillion in assets divested by 2022 (1,500+ institutions). Major universities, pension funds, sovereign wealth funds divested. But: actual fossil fuel investment simultaneously increased globally — divestment shifted ownership without reducing production. The movement's reputational and social license impact is documented; its direct emissions impact is not. Score 3 — cultural/institutional shift without binding emissions reduction.

progress_markers.event: Bill McKibben's 'Global Warming's Terrifying New Math' (Rolling Stone, July 2012 — retroactively the founding document) establishes 'carbon bubble' concept: fossil fuels already booked exceed safe carbon budget by 5x
November 2012: 350.org launches 'Do the Math' tour. Swarthmore College students launch divestment campaign against their endowment — the first campus divestment campaign to gain mainstream attention
Stanford University faculty vote to recommend divestment from coal. 17 US colleges have active campaigns. The campus model spreads internationally
Stanford University divests from coal (April 2014) — first major research university. Rockefeller Brothers Fund divests (September 2014) — symbolically powerful given Rockefeller oil money origins
Norway sovereign wealth fund (world's largest, $900B) divests from coal (June 2015). Most significant institutional divestment signal to date. Germany's Allianz, Australia's ANU follow
Paris Agreement (December 2015) provides policy context for fiduciary arguments. 'Stranded assets' concept (fossil fuel reserves that cannot be burned) gains traction with financial regulators
Total divested assets: $8 trillion. UK pension funds, Dutch pension giants (ABP eventually, after campus pressure), French insurance companies announcing divestment
University of California system ($126B endowment) announces full fossil fuel divestment. Harvard announces divestment (September 2021) after years of campus protest
Total divested assets: $14 trillion. Norway full sovereign wealth fund ($1.3T) divestment from fossil fuel upstream operations (September 2021)
Total divested assets: $40+ trillion (1,500+ institutions). The movement has gone mainstream — major banks and asset managers now have ESG fossil fuel reduction targets
ESG backlash in US: Republican-led states target 'ESG investing' as political ideology; some pension funds forced to re-invest in fossil fuels under state law pressure

Global fossil fuel investment reaches record high ($1+ trillion in 2023) — divestment has shifted ownership but not reduced production. Production gap between Paris targets and actual investment widening

COP28 (Dubai, 2023) 'transitioning away from fossil fuels' language represents normative shift; but no binding production cap. Divestment movement credited with making this language politically achievable

lead_orgs.name: 350.org
lead_orgs.description: Founded by Bill McKibben; central hub for the divestment movement globally. Campaigns in 188 countries

lead_orgs.name: Fossil Free (350.org campaign)
lead_orgs.description: Specific divestment campaign platform; coordinated campus, city, and institutional campaigns globally

lead_orgs.name: Carbon Tracker Initiative
lead_orgs.description: Provided the financial analysis (carbon bubble, stranded assets) that gave divestment campaigns a fiduciary argument

funders: Rockefeller Brothers Fund (ironically, the first major funder)
celebrities: Desmond Tutu ('apartheid of our age' framing)
government_champions: Mark Carney (Bank of England, then FSB — 'stranded assets' and 'climate risk' financial regulatory discourse)
international: TCFD (Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures — Mark Carney)

71.what_actually_changed: $40+ trillion in committed divestment by 1,500+ institutions. Fossil fuel companies' cost of capital increased. Social license for new fossil fuel infrastructure projects significantly reduced. Harvard divestment (2021) after 8 years of student protest represented the ultimate symbolic victory. Shell, BP, and others changed their public narrative (net zero commitments) partly under divestment pressure. What has not changed: global fossil fuel production, which increased to record levels in 2023. The movement's most significant impact may be what has NOT been built (new fossil fuel infrastructure that faced opposition from investors) rather than what existing reserves were sold.

72.name: Bangladesh Climate Litigation / Youth v. Government
72.outcome_summary: Ongoing as of 2025. Landmark case filed by youth climate activists challenging coal plant approvals on constitutional grounds. Case precedent-building phase. Bangladesh represents the starkest case of extreme climate vulnerability (30M+ people at sea-level rise risk) combined with continued domestic fossil fuel development. Score 2 reflects partial win in establishing the legal framework, with policy outcome pending.

progress_markers.event: Urgenda (Netherlands) case filed — providing international template for climate litigation that would inspire Bangladesh and others
Bangladesh government approves Rampal coal power plant (near Sundarbans mangrove forest — UNESCO-designated, one of world's largest). Despite massive civil society opposition
Bangladesh announces ten new coal power plants in National Electricity Plan. Climate activists note contradiction: most climate-vulnerable nation building most carbon-intensive infrastructure
Bangladesh Youth Climate Action and human rights organizations begin developing legal arguments. Sabin Center for Climate Change Law (Columbia) provides international legal expertise
Bangladesh signs Bridgetown Initiative (climate finance reform) and declares itself climate vulnerable — diplomatic recognition of vulnerability while building coal plants domestically
Bangladesh cancels 10 planned coal projects citing financing difficulties and 'global coal phase-out' — partially responding to international pressure and financial constraints

Youth climate activists file landmark constitutional challenge: coal plant approvals violate rights to life and livelihood guaranteed in Bangladesh constitution, disproportionately harm climate-vulnerable communities
High Court issues initial rule requiring government to show cause. Bangladesh's worst flooding (2022-2023 monsoons) provides ongoing visual evidence of harm

Case proceeding through courts. August 2024 student revolution (unrelated to climate — overthrew Hasina government over quota system) creates political uncertainty but new government potentially more open to climate arguments

New interim Bangladesh government under Muhammad Yunus signals interest in climate justice framing. Case continues. Rampal plant now operational — litigation seeking prospective rather than retrospective relief

lead_orgs.name: Bangladesh Youth Climate Action (BYCA)
lead_orgs.description: Student-led organization; primary petitioners in climate litigation

lead_orgs.name: Human Rights and Peace for Bangladesh (HRPB)
lead_orgs.description: Legal NGO supporting the litigation

lead_orgs.name: Bangladesh Legal Aid and Services Trust (BLAST)
lead_orgs.description: Legal support organization with climate case expertise

funders: Sabin Center for Climate Change Law (technical support)
government_champions: Muhammad Yunus (new interim PM, 2024 — more climate-open than Hasina government)

villains: Adani Group (Godda coal power plant — supplying Rampal)
Bangladesh Power Development Board (state utility, coal plant operator)

international: UN Human Rights Council (Bangladesh climate vulnerability documentation)
ICJ (Vanuatu advisory opinion — potential precedent for Bangladesh case)

72.what_actually_changed: Bangladesh government cancelled 10 planned coal power plants in 2022 (financial/political context but climate pressure contributed). Constitutional framework for climate rights being established through litigation. The case's most significant contribution may be to Bangladesh's constitutional jurisprudence — establishing that climate harm is a constitutional rights violation, providing a framework for future challenges. Physical conditions continue deteriorating: 2022-2023 floods displaced millions; Sundarbans facing accelerating damage.

73.name: Pacific Islands: Climate Change as Existential Threat
73.outcome_summary: Loss and Damage Fund established at COP27 (2022) — historic first: wealthy nations acknowledge financial responsibility for climate harm. 1.5°C target embedded in Paris Agreement partly due to Pacific leadership. Vanuatu ICJ advisory opinion campaign (case #100) represents the movement's latest legal escalation. Score 3 — major cultural/normative shift (1.5°C language, loss and damage) but no physical protection achieved. Islands continue to flood.

progress_markers.event: Alliance of Small Island States (AOSIS) founded — 44 low-lying coastal and small island countries. First time small island nations organized as a bloc in climate negotiations
UNFCCC enters into force. AOSIS submits first-ever proposal for binding emissions reduction targets — ignored by major emitters. 20% by 2005 proposal rejected

November 2009: Maldives cabinet meeting underwater (scuba gear) — environment minister Mohamed Aslam signs declaration calling for global emissions cuts. Globally viral image. One of the most effective pieces of environmental performance activism ever
COP15 Copenhagen collapse. Pacific Islands delegation weeps as 2°C (not 1.5°C) is the only surviving target. Lumumba Di-Aping (Sudan) calls the Copenhagen Accord 'a suicide pact'
Yeb Saño (Philippines) weeps at Warsaw COP13 following Typhoon Hainan — most watched climate speech in COP history. Fasts for 2 weeks during negotiations. '1.5 to stay alive' becomes defining slogan
Kiribati President Anote Tong purchases land in Fiji as 'land bank' for climate migration — first state to do so. Reframes climate change as displacement not just adaptation

Paris Agreement includes '1.5°C' language — direct result of Pacific/AOSIS/vulnerable nations pressure. Without Pacific lobbying, Paris would have been 2°C only. Considered Pacific's greatest diplomatic achievement

IPCC Special Report on 1.5°C (October 2018): the difference between 1.5°C and 2°C is existential for Pacific Islands (coral bleaching, sea level rise). Pacific advocacy had made this the key scientific question
Pacific Climate Warriors ('We are not drowning, we are fighting') — youth movement reframes Pacific identity from victim to fighter. Stand.earth and 350 Pacific partnership

COP27 Sharm el-Sheikh: Loss and Damage Fund established after 30 years of Pacific/AOSIS demands. Wealthy nations formally acknowledge financial responsibility for climate harm they caused to vulnerable countries
Vanuatu leads UNGA resolution requesting ICJ advisory opinion on states' climate obligations (132 co-sponsors). The most ambitious legal escalation in Pacific climate advocacy
Tuvalu Prime Minister Kausea Natano signs 'Falepili Union' with Australia — first climate-migration treaty. Australia offers pathway for Tuvaluans to migrate as climate refugees
Tuvalu announces 'digital nation' — first country establishing digital statehood in preparation for physical loss. Extraordinary reimagining of sovereignty
September 2024: ICJ hearings in The Hague on climate obligations — 98 countries argue, largest state participation in ICJ history. Advisory opinion expected 2025
COP28 Dubai: Loss and Damage Fund operationalized but initial capitalization ($700M) described as 'rounding error' by Pacific advocates. Fund mechanism exists; funding inadequate

lead_orgs.name: Alliance of Small Island States (AOSIS)
lead_orgs.description: 44-member coalition; the most consistent and principled voice in climate negotiations for 30+ years

lead_orgs.name: Pacific Climate Warriors (350 Pacific)
lead_orgs.description: Youth-led movement reframing Pacific identity from victim to fighter. Stand.earth partnership

lead_orgs.name: Vanuatu government
lead_orgs.description: Led the ICJ advisory opinion campaign; consistent multilateral champion

celebrities: Yeb Saño (Philippines climate delegate — tearful Warsaw speech reached 100M+ viewers)
Marshallese poet Kathy Jetñil-Kijiner (performed at COP21)
government_champions: Anote Tong (Kiribati President, most prominent Pacific climate leader 2003-2016)
Enele Sopoaga (Tuvalu PM, most forceful negotiating voice)

73.what_actually_changed: The '1.5°C' target in Paris Agreement is the Pacific's most significant diplomatic achievement — without Pacific pressure, it would have been 2°C only. Loss and Damage Fund (COP27) is the first structural acknowledgment of climate reparations. Tuvalu's Australia migration treaty is the first climate-migration legal framework. ICJ advisory opinion (expected 2025) may establish the most authoritative legal statement on climate obligations ever. What has NOT changed: emissions trajectories, sea levels, coral bleaching. Pacific islands will be uninhabitable within 50-100 years under current trajectories regardless of these diplomatic wins.

74.name: Global Anti-Apartheid Movement
74.outcome_summary: Complete success: apartheid dismantled through negotiation 1990-1994; Nelson Mandela freed 1990; first democratic elections April 1994. One of the most successful long-term international solidarity campaigns in history. The combination of armed resistance (ANC), internal mass organizing (UDF), international economic pressure (divestment, sanctions), and cultural boycotts demonstrated the multi-track model at scale. Score 5 — full sustained systemic change.

progress_markers.event: National Party wins South African elections; apartheid system formalized. ANC (founded 1912) begins systematic resistance
Sharpeville Massacre (March 21, 1960): 69 anti-pass-law protesters killed by police. International condemnation. ANC and PAC banned
Nelson Mandela imprisoned (Robben Island). Anti-Apartheid Movement founded in UK (1959). International isolation campaign begins
South Africa expelled from Olympics. Commonwealth Games boycotts begin. Sports boycott becomes most visceral public face of international pressure
Soweto Uprising (June 16, 1976): students protest Afrikaans instruction; 176+ killed. Global outrage. Sam Nzima's photograph of Hector Pieterson dying becomes iconic
Steve Biko tortured to death in police detention. International outrage. UN mandatory arms embargo (1977) — first mandatory UN sanctions in history
Student divestment campaigns begin on US campuses (Columbia University, Michigan). 'Apartheid' becomes globally understood term
'Free Nelson Mandela' by The Specials reaches #1 in UK (June 1984). Wembley Concert for Mandela (1988, 72,000 in stadium, 600M TV viewers). Cultural isolation made visceral
State of emergency in South Africa. Township TV footage reaches Western audiences despite censorship attempts. US Comprehensive Anti-Apartheid Act (1986) — passed over Reagan's veto
US CAAA (1986): comprehensive sanctions on South Africa; divestment by US institutions required. Symbolic power: Congress overrides sitting President to impose sanctions

UDF (United Democratic Front) inside South Africa coordinates mass resistance. Internal and external resistance increasingly synchronized. ANC's Oliver Tambo's global diplomacy reaching peak effectiveness
Wembley Stadium Nelson Mandela 70th Birthday Tribute Concert — 72,000 audience, 600M+ TV viewers in 67 countries. 'Free Mandela' becomes the most widely heard political demand in pop history

F.W. de Klerk elected; begins behind-the-scenes negotiations with Mandela. Global sanctions and economic pressure reaching critical mass — South African business community breaks with apartheid government
February 2, 1990: de Klerk unbans ANC, PAC, SACP. February 11: Nelson Mandela walks free after 27 years. Global celebration
April 27, 1994: First democratic elections. 19.7 million South Africans vote; ANC wins 62.6%. Nelson Mandela inaugurated May 10 as first democratically elected President of South Africa

lead_orgs.name: African National Congress (ANC)
lead_orgs.description: Founded 1912; the organizational backbone of the anti-apartheid struggle. Oliver Tambo's exile diplomacy was central to the international campaign

lead_orgs.name: Anti-Apartheid Movement (AAM — UK)
lead_orgs.description: Founded 1959; coordinated the international solidarity movement in the UK and Commonwealth

lead_orgs.name: United Democratic Front (UDF — South Africa)
lead_orgs.description: Internal resistance coalition 1983-1991; coordinated mass action inside South Africa during the state of emergency

funders: Nordic governments (Sweden, Norway, Denmark — early funders of ANC in exile)
UN General Assembly (1962 resolution supporting divestment)
celebrities: The Specials, Peter Gabriel, Simple Minds (cultural boycott / 'Free Mandela' anthem)
government_champions: Olof Palme (Swedish PM — ANC champion assassinated 1986)

villains: IBM and other US tech companies (supplied apartheid government)
Barclays Bank (major divestment campaign target — withdrew 1986)
allies: Barclays Bank (withdrew from South Africa 1986 under UK student/church pressure)
Major US corporations that divested 1986-1988 after CAAA

international: UN General Assembly (mandatory arms embargo 1977, multiple resolutions)

74.what_actually_changed: Apartheid system dismantled. One person one vote democracy established. All apartheid-era laws repealed. Truth and Reconciliation Commission (1996-1998) — partially successful accountability mechanism. Constitutional protection of LGBTQ rights (first in Africa). What has NOT fully changed: economic apartheid persists — South Africa remains among world's most unequal economies (GINI ~0.63). ANC governance has disappointed on corruption and service delivery. Mandela's legacy overshadows structural failures. But the political transformation was complete — apartheid as a legal system ended. The divestment model here directly influenced fossil fuel divestment 30 years later.

75.name: Haiti Earthquake Response → Supply Chain Critique / NGO Accountability
75.outcome_summary: The '6 houses for $500 million' ProPublica/NPR investigation (2015) became the defining accountability story for international humanitarian NGOs. Red Cross CEO resigned. Debate about aid industrial complex entered mainstream. But: no structural change to international humanitarian system. Haiti descended into gang-controlled chaos by 2022-2024. Score 1: Awareness only — the campaign surfaced NGO accountability as an issue but produced no structural change.

progress_markers.event: January 12, 2010: 7.0 magnitude earthquake. 220,000 killed (disputed; possibly 100,000-316,000). 300,000 injured. 1.5 million displaced. Port-au-Prince devastated
International response: $13 billion pledged — largest humanitarian response in history at that point. Text-to-donate technology raises $43M for Red Cross in 48 hours. 'We are all Haitian' moment
October 2010: Cholera outbreak — traced to UN peacekeepers (MINUSTAH). UN denies responsibility for years. 820,000 infected, 10,000+ dead. Accountability gap exposed early
One year later: 800,000 still in tent camps. Reconstruction promises largely unmet. Interim Haiti Recovery Commission (co-chaired by Bill Clinton) dysfunction documented by journalists
$6 billion in post-quake aid delivered; living conditions remain catastrophic. Aid system's parallel structures (bypassing Haitian government) criticized by local civil society

June 2015: ProPublica/NPR investigation publishes 'How the Red Cross Raised Half a Billion Dollars for Haiti and Built 6 Homes.' The '6 houses' story goes globally viral — devastating accountability story
Red Cross CEO Gail McGovern faces congressional testimony. Red Cross forced to release some financial information previously withheld. American Red Cross donations drop 24% following the story
Broader NGO accountability debate: effectiveness, overhead ratios, 'white saviorism' in aid. Organizations including Oxfam publish 'localisation' commitments
Haiti presidential elections disputed; political instability accelerates. Clinton Foundation's Haiti projects also scrutinized during US presidential election — mixed accountability outcomes
July 7, 2021: President Jovenel Moïse assassinated. August 14: 7.2 magnitude earthquake (72% as powerful as 2010) hits southwestern Haiti. International response smaller than 2010
Haiti descends into gang-controlled chaos: Ariel Henry government absent; gangs control 80%+ of Port-au-Prince by 2023. 'G9' and other gang federations effectively govern parts of the country
UN Security Council authorizes multinational security mission (Kenyan-led). Kenya Supreme Court initially blocks deployment; eventually proceeds 2024
Haiti crisis: 5M+ facing acute food insecurity. Gang violence kills 5,600+ in 2023. International security mission (MSS) deployed but insufficient. Full state collapse ongoing

lead_orgs.name: American Red Cross
lead_orgs.description: Raised $500M for Haiti; later subject of devastating accountability journalism. Most prominent case study in NGO failure

lead_orgs.name: Bill Clinton (UN Special Envoy for Haiti)
lead_orgs.description: Co-chaired Interim Haiti Recovery Commission; Clinton Foundation ran Haiti programs — both became accountability targets

lead_orgs.name: ProPublica/NPR (Justin Elliott, Laura Sullivan)
lead_orgs.description: Journalists whose investigation ('6 houses') created the defining accountability moment for international humanitarian response

celebrities: Wyclef Jean (Haitian-American musician; ran Yéle Haiti charity — separately exposed for financial mismanagement)
Sean Penn (established J/P Haitian Relief Organization — more accountable model; operated in Haiti for years)

villains: Digicel (Irish telecommunications monopoly in Haiti; Denis O'Brien donated but also profited enormously from post-quake Haiti)

international: MINUSTAH (UN peacekeepers who introduced cholera — UN eventually acknowledged and apologized in 2016)
IMF (structural adjustment conditions attached to Haiti reconstruction loans)

75.what_actually_changed: The '6 houses' story produced: Red Cross forced to increase financial transparency; congressional oversight hearings; some NGO sector soul-searching on accountability and effectiveness metrics. Several organizations published 'localization' commitments. The 'overhead ratio' debate produced GiveWell and effective altruism as accountability frameworks (though separately). What did NOT change: the international humanitarian system's architecture (foreign NGOs controlling funding over local organizations), Haiti's structural crisis, or the accountability-free zone for humanitarian failures. Haiti's 2024 total state collapse occurred while international humanitarian organizations continued operating inside the country — the ultimate test of the 'aid industrial complex' critique.

76.name: Uganda Anti-LGBTQ Law — International Response
76.outcome_summary: Despite unprecedented international economic pressure — World Bank suspended lending (Aug 2023), US visa restrictions, EU aid reviews — Uganda's Anti-Homosexuality Act 2023 remained law as of 2025. Uganda's Constitutional Court struck some provisions (April 2024) but declined to invalidate the law entirely. World Bank resumed lending June 2025 citing 'mitigation measures', which activists criticized as capitulation. The law is enforced; LGBTQ Ugandans face increased police violence, evictions, and social persecution. HIV prevention services fell 40%+ after enactment. The international campaign demonstrated that external pressure without strong domestic organizational capacity is insufficient to change laws where governments have domestic political incentives to maintain them.

progress_markers.event: Scott Lively and American evangelical organizations actively lobby Ugandan parliament to pass 'Kill the Gays' bill — establishing external anti-LGBTQ political infrastructure in Uganda
Uganda's original Anti-Homosexuality Bill introduced with death penalty provisions; international condemnation forces temporary shelving
David Kato, Uganda's first openly gay activist, murdered; Ugandan tabloids had published his name and address in 'Hang Them' feature

Anti-Homosexuality Act 2014 signed by President Museveni. World Bank, US, UK, Denmark suspend ~$110M in aid. Uganda's Constitutional Court strikes down law on procedural grounds (August) — reprieve but movement suppressed
New Anti-Homosexuality Bill introduced; broader than 2014 version with expanded 'promotion' clauses targeting civil society organizations and health workers
Parliament passes Anti-Homosexuality Act 2023 (March). Death penalty for 'aggravated homosexuality', life imprisonment for same-sex relations, 20 years for 'promotion'
President Museveni signs Act May 26, 2023 despite UN Human Rights Commissioner calling it 'devastating and deeply disturbing'
World Bank announces suspension of new public lending to Uganda (August 2023) — $300M+ in projects paused. US imposes visa restrictions. EU reviews budget support
International AIDS Society documents 40%+ drop in LGBTQ Ugandans seeking HIV services. Sexual Minorities Uganda (underground) documents arrests, evictions, violence
Uganda Constitutional Court nullifies some provisions (April 2024) — narrows 'aggravated' definition — but declines to strike down the law. Life imprisonment for same-sex relations remains
World Bank resumes lending to Uganda (June 2025) citing 'mitigation measures' — widely criticized by LGBTQ activists as capitulation. Law remains enforced

77.name: Colombia: Peace Accord Plebiscite and Implementation
77.outcome_summary: The 2016 peace accord produced genuine transformation: ~7,000 FARC combatants demobilized, ex-FARC party (Comunes) allowed to contest elections, and Special Jurisdiction for Peace (JEP) established as transitional justice mechanism. Santos won Nobel Peace Prize. However, Duque's government (2018-2022) severely undermined implementation — rural reform stalled, 300+ social leaders murdered annually, FARC dissidents (Estado Mayor Central, Segunda Marquetalia) rearm with 3,000-5,000 fighters. Petro's 'Total Peace' agenda (2022-) relaunched implementation. Kroc Institute estimates 25-30% implementation of accord. The 52-year FARC war formally ended; structural violence continues through new actors. Score 3: institutional shift, no binding structural change.

progress_markers.event: FARC founded. 52 years of civil war, 220,000+ killed, 7 million displaced — Latin America's longest internal armed conflict
Secret peace negotiations begin in Havana. Cuba and Norway as guarantors. Santos takes political risk for peace
Agreement on transitional justice: Special Jurisdiction for Peace (JEP) — truth-telling with reduced sentences for acknowledgment of crimes
August: Peace accord signed in Cartagena. Santos awarded Nobel Peace Prize October 2016
October 2: Plebiscite fails — 50.2% No (49.8% Yes). Uribe's disinformation campaign about 'gender ideology' and FARC impunity swings narrow defeat
December: Revised accord ratified by Congress. FARC begins demobilizing into transition zones
~7,000 FARC combatants demobilize; 8,994 weapons collected and verified by UN. FARC party Comunes contests elections
Duque (Uribe ally) wins presidency — openly hostile to accord. Slows rural reform, cuts reintegration budget, undermines JEP. Ex-combatant assassination rate rises
Iván Márquez and 'Jesús Santrich' announce return to arms (Segunda Marquetalia) — implementation failures cited. ~2,000 fighters rearm
300-400+ social leaders, human rights defenders, and ex-combatants murdered annually. Land reform nearly non-existent. Rural security vacuum filled by FARC dissidents, ELN, and criminal groups
Petro elected as first leftist president (former M-19 member). 'Total Peace' agenda: negotiations with ELN, Estado Mayor Central, smaller criminal groups
JEP produces first landmark verdicts acknowledging FARC crimes against humanity. Truth Commission report documents 450,000+ victims in detail
Accord implementation at 25-30% (Kroc Institute). Rural reform minimal. But formal armed conflict with FARC generation ended — major historical achievement despite incomplete peace

78.name: Bangladesh 1971 Genocide Awareness
78.outcome_summary: Bangladesh achieved independence (December 16, 1971 — Pakistani surrender). George Harrison's Concert for Bangladesh (August 1, 1971) was the first major rock charity concert, raising $12 million and establishing the celebrity-as-crisis-witness model used in every subsequent humanitarian campaign (Live Aid, Band Aid, etc.). But: Pakistan has never recognized the 1971 atrocities as genocide. No Pakistani military officers faced international criminal accountability. Bangladesh's domestic International Crimes Tribunal (2010-) prosecuted local Razakar collaborators — international due process concerns raised. Sheikh Hasina's government was toppled by mass student protests (August 2024), creating uncertainty about ICT convictions. Score 2: independence achieved (WIN); international accountability campaign produced partial wins at best.

progress_markers.event: March 25: Pakistani military Operation Searchlight begins. 300,000-3 million killed; 200,000-400,000 women raped; 10 million refugees flee to India
US government (Nixon/Kissinger) blocks UN action — Pakistan is Cold War ally. Blood Telegram from US Consul General Archer Blood condemns US inaction as genocide

August 1: Concert for Bangladesh at Madison Square Garden. George Harrison and Ravi Shankar; Bob Dylan, Eric Clapton, Ringo Starr. First major rock charity concert. $12 million raised. First use of celebrity concert as crisis advocacy tool
December 16: Pakistan surrenders to Indian forces and Mukti Bahini. Bangladesh independence. 93,000 Pakistani POWs captured
Simla Agreement: India-Pakistan deal repatriates POWs without war crimes trials. No international accountability. Bangladesh excluded from key negotiations
Bangladesh establishes International Crimes Tribunal (ICT) — domestic tribunal to prosecute Razakar and al-Badr collaborators
Shahbag protests (February 2013): massive youth demonstrations demanding death penalty for convicted war criminals. ICT issues multiple convictions
Hefazat-e-Islam counter-protests; ICT proceedings criticized internationally for due process concerns. Multiple Jamaat-e-Islami leaders executed following convictions
50th independence anniversary. Canada, several European parliaments pass non-binding genocide recognition motions. Pakistan still refuses genocide characterization

August: Sheikh Hasina toppled by mass student protests (quota reform uprising). Military caretaker government. Fate of ICT convictions and genocide recognition agenda uncertain in political transition

79.name: India: Right to Information Movement
79.outcome_summary: The Right to Information Act (2005) is one of the most consequential transparency laws enacted in any democracy. By 2015, 6+ million RTI applications were filed annually; the law enabled hundreds of thousands of corruption accountability cases and was extensively used by investigative journalists. However, success made it a target: 80+ RTI activists killed 2005-2022 for filing inconvenient applications; the 2019 RTI Amendment Act (Modi's BJP government) weakened Information Commissioners' independence by making them government-removable and reducing tenure security. Courts upheld the amendments. PM CARES Fund was excluded from RTI scrutiny. Score 4: major policy win, now partially vulnerable to executive pressure.

progress_markers.event: MKSS (Mazdoor Kisan Shakti Sangathan) founded by Aruna Roy and Nikhil De in Rajasthan — begins 'jan sunwais' (public hearings) demanding government muster rolls for wage workers
Rajasthan state RTI campaign — 'Show us the records' becomes mass movement among rural workers
National Campaign for People's Right to Information (NCPRI) formed — national coalition of civil society organizations
Aruna Roy wins Ramon Magsaysay Award — international visibility for RTI movement
Freedom of Information Act passed — weak version with many exemptions. Civil society rejects as inadequate, continues campaigning
UPA coalition government (Congress + Left) wins election — both had supported stronger RTI. Political window opens
Right to Information Act passed June 2005 — 30-day response requirement, Public Information Officers designated, Central Information Commission with judicial-equivalent powers
6+ million RTI applications filed annually. RTI cited in Supreme Court judgments. Commonwealth Games corruption exposed via RTI. Model studied globally
RTI activists begin being systematically murdered for filing inconvenient applications — land records, mining, PDS fraud. 67 killed 2005-2015 (CHRI data)

RTI Amendment Act passed (August 2019) — reduces Chief Information Commissioner tenure from 5 years to government's discretion; salary linked to government scale. Effectively makes commissioners government-removable
Supreme Court upholds 2019 amendments. PM CARES Fund ruled not subject to RTI. CICs increasingly reluctant to order compliance by central government departments
7+ million applications/year — mechanism operational but institutional independence weakened. Electoral bonds excluded from RTI scrutiny until Supreme Court struck them down (2024) on other grounds

80.name: Congo: Conflict Minerals / Raise Hope for Congo
80.outcome_summary: Section 1502 of Dodd-Frank (2010) established the first US law requiring supply chain transparency for conflict minerals (tin, tantalum, tungsten, gold — 3TG from DRC). A certified smelter program (RMAP) emerged; 300+ smelters certified. EU Conflict Minerals Regulation followed (effective 2021). However: actual impact on DRC conflict was limited — M23 resurgence in 2022-2024 demonstrated structural conflict drivers unresolved. Trump's 2017 'no action' guidance suspended enforcement temporarily. Battery metals (cobalt, lithium) emerged as a new conflict mineral vector outside the 3TG framework.
