81.name: Philippines: Marcos Disinformation vs. Democracy Movement [FAILED TIP]
81.outcome_summary: One of history's most consequential disinformation victories: Bongbong Marcos won the 2022 Philippine presidential election with 58.7% despite documented crimes of the Marcos dictatorship (10,000+ tortured, 3,000+ killed, $5-10 billion stolen, 1972-1986). A systematic 6-year social media campaign rehabilitated the Marcos legacy among a generation with no memory of martial law. The 'pink revolution' pro-Robredo rallies were among the largest in Philippine history — and lost by a landslide. Maria Ressa (Nobel laureate) and Rappler were forced to cease operations December 2023. Rodrigo Duterte was arrested in Manila for ICC proceedings (March 2025) — partial accountability for drug war deaths. Score 0: democracy movement definitively defeated; disinformation won.

82.name: Turkey Gezi Park Protests
82.outcome_summary: Gezi Park was saved from demolition — the immediate demand was won. But Erdoğan weaponized the protests to justify security crackdowns, consolidate his conservative base, and prosecute civil society for years. The iconic 'woman in the red dress' photograph became globally viral, but Ceyda Sungur and hundreds of others were subsequently prosecuted. Osman Kavala (cultural philanthropist) was imprisoned from 2017 and received a life sentence in 2022 — Turkey's refusal to comply with ECHR release order led to the worst member state confrontation in Council of Europe history. Turkey's democratic trajectory continued downward; the 2016 coup and subsequent purges eliminated whatever political opening Gezi created. Score 2: partial win (park saved), broader political loss.

83.name: Colombia: Biodiversity Conservation at COP16
83.outcome_summary: Colombia's hosting of COP16 (Cali, October 2024) produced genuine institutional achievements: the historic DSI (Digital Sequence Information) benefit-sharing agreement — first mechanism ensuring developing countries receive revenue when commercial entities use genetic data from their biodiversity (estimated $100M+/year). Record 23,000+ civil society attendees. Indigenous peoples formally integrated into biodiversity governance for the first time (Article 8(j) subsidiary body). However, the plenary ran out of time without completing all agenda items — a resumed session was required. Under Petro, Colombia reduced Amazon deforestation by 40% vs. Duque. Structural pressures (coca, illegal mining, FARC dissidents) persist. Score 3: important institutional and normative shifts, no fully binding implementation framework yet.

84.name: Bhopal Disaster — Ongoing Accountability
84.outcome_summary: Forty years of one of the most sustained corporate accountability campaigns in history has produced almost no meaningful justice. The 1984 Union Carbide gas leak killed 3,500-15,000 immediately; 500,000+ suffer long-term health effects. The 1989 settlement — $470 million — averaged ~$600 per victim for lifetime injury or death. Warren Anderson (CEO) died in 2014 having never been extradited. Dow Chemical (acquired Union Carbide 2001) refuses all liability for cleanup or additional compensation. The 2010 Indian court conviction — 2-year sentences for local executives — was universally condemned as inadequate. Third-generation birth defects documented. Site remains contaminated 40 years on. Score 1: awareness maintained for 40 years; zero adequate accountability achieved.

85.name: Thailand: Save the Ocean / Marium the Dugong
85.outcome_summary: Marium, a baby dugong rescued and hand-reared by Thai marine biologists, died August 2019 after ingesting plastic waste. Her death created national grief and was a key trigger for Thailand's plastic bag ban (January 2020) and subsequent single-use plastic bans (2022). Thailand's plastic policy trajectory aligned with international trends but Marium's story provided the emotional catalyst and specific political cover. However, plastic pollution in Thai seas remains severe; tourism-dependent coastal areas continue to face crises; dugong population remains critically endangered (~250 individuals in Thailand). Score 3: genuine policy change, awareness elevated, structural plastic production unchanged.

86.name: Global Anti-Dam Movement / Narmada
86.outcome_summary: The Narmada Bachao Andolan achieved a landmark international victory when the World Bank withdrew from Sardar Sarovar funding (October 1993) — the first time ever the Bank withdrew from a major project under civil society pressure. This produced the World Bank Inspection Panel (1993), which remains operational today as a global accountability innovation. The World Commission on Dams (2000) established international standards still referenced globally. But: India built the dam with national funding. Supreme Court allowed multiple height increases. 320,000+ people were displaced with deeply inadequate rehabilitation. The dam is operational. Score 2: international accountability standards permanently changed; the dam was built, people were displaced.

87.name: Wangari Maathai: Karura Forest Defense
87.outcome_summary: Karura Forest — 1,000+ hectares of indigenous forest in central Nairobi — was saved from cronyism development and is now a protected, fully accessible urban forest park serving 10,000+ visitors daily. Maathai's January 1999 tree-planting action (met with beatings before international diplomats) created the visual that reversed the land allocations. The campaign contributed to political pressure ending Moi's regime. Kenya's 2010 constitution includes environmental rights. Wangari Maathai won the 2004 Nobel Peace Prize (first time awarded to an environmental activist). Score 4: the forest was saved, the institutional gains were embedded in constitutional law, and the movement created a globally replicated model.

88.name: Uganda: Stop Kony / KONY 2012
88.outcome_summary: KONY 2012 reached 100 million views in 6 days (fastest video to that milestone at the time), but 'Cover the Night' (April 20, 2012) failed; director Jason Russell's public breakdown consumed the cycle. 89.name: Indonesia: Peatland Fires / Haze Crisis Accountability
89.outcome_summary: The 2015 Indonesian peatland fires killed ~100,000 people prematurely (Harvard/Columbia study), emitted ~1.75 billion tons CO2, and caused hazardous air across Malaysia and Singapore. 90.name: Mexico: Women's Movement and Day Without Women

92.outcome_summary: Hong Kong 2019 had near-perfect SIPM conditions (24/25) and achieved its immediate stated demand — the Extradition Bill was withdrawn September 2019. Then the National Security Law was imposed June 30, 2020. Mass arrests followed: 10,000+ arrested 2019-2023; 47 pro-democracy figures convicted (2024, most imprisoned); Jimmy Lai on trial for life; Apple Daily and Stand News forced to close; pro-democracy parties disbanded; electoral system overhauled to admit only 'patriots.' Hong Kong's democratic character is functionally destroyed. The movement's visual legacy (Lennon Walls, umbrellas, be-water tactics) traveled to Belarus, Thailand, Myanmar, and beyond — but did not protect local participants. Score 1: immediate demand won; everything else was taken and more.

93.name: Russia Anti-War Movement [FAILED TIP — impossible conditions]
93.outcome_summary: Russia's anti-war movement was crushed within weeks of the February 24, 2022 Ukraine invasion. 19,000+ arrested in first year; 'discrediting the military' law imposed up to 15-year sentences for anti-war speech. Independent media (Novaya Gazeta, Dozhd, Echo Moskvy) closed or fled. Alexei Navalny died in Arctic prison colony (February 2024). 700,000-900,000 anti-war Russians emigrated in 2022. The Anti-Corruption Foundation operates from exile. Ukraine war continues through 2025 with Russia controlling ~20% of Ukraine. Score 0: fully suppressed; SIPM score of 13/25 predicts the failure accurately.

94.name: US No Kings Anti-Trump Resistance
94.outcome_summary: The anti-Trump resistance of Trump's second term represents historically sustained mobilization — the 'No Kings' protests and predecessors may be the largest sustained US movement since Vietnam. Federal courts have blocked hundreds of executive actions via injunctions. Some Republican senators have broken with the administration on specific issues. However: structural policy changes (EPA gutting, Paris Agreement withdrawal, IRA rollback, immigration enforcement) have proceeded. DOGE cuts to federal agencies continue. The 2026 midterm elections are the proximate institutional converting mechanism. Score 2: partial wins via courts, ongoing — outcome TBD by 2026.

Supreme Court (6-3 conservative) overturns some injunctions. Paris Agreement withdrawal complete. IRA renewable subsidies partially clawed back via reconciliation
2026 midterm elections approaching. Voter registration surges. Whether sustained mobilization converts to electoral change is the defining question of the movement's success

95.name: Sudan Revolution Phase II — Civil War [FAILED TIP]
95.outcome_summary: The April 2023 war between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and Rapid Support Forces (RSF) produced the world's largest displacement crisis by 2024: 12 million+ displaced (2M+ refugees), famine in multiple regions, and RSF mass atrocities in Darfur described by UN officials as genocide. The civilian democratic movement that ousted Bashir (2019) was caught between two military factions competing for sole control. All ceasefire negotiations (Jeddah, Geneva, Cairo) collapsed. As of 2025: no peace deal, no functioning central government. Sudan is the most catastrophic 'after the revolution' failure in this dataset.

progress_markers.event: Revolution ousts Bashir (April 2019). But: SAF-RSF military council forms. Sit-in massacre (June 3): 60-100 killed. Fragile civilian-military sovereignty council established
October 25, 2021: SAF-RSF joint coup — civilian transitional government dissolved. PM Hamdok arrested. International community suspends Sudan from African Union
Framework Agreement (December 2022) — SAF and civilian factions agree to civilian governance return. RSF agrees. But negotiations stall over RSF integration timeline into national army
April 15: War begins — SAF and RSF fight for Khartoum. Fighter jets and heavy weapons in the capital. Within days: entire country destabilized. All embassies evacuate
May-June: RSF captures most of Khartoum. El Geneina (West Darfur) massacres — thousands killed; widespread rape. UN officials describe as genocide
Jeddah talks (May-October 2023): US/Saudi-brokered ceasefire. Both sides sign, violate immediately. No lasting ceasefire achieved
8 million internally displaced + 2 million refugees. IPC Phase 5 famine in Darfur. El Fasher (last major Darfur city not under RSF) under siege
Geneva talks (August 2024) collapse without agreement. RSF announces intent to form parallel government. African Union, IGAD, Qatar all fail to mediate
12+ million displaced. 150,000+ killed. Largest displacement crisis globally. International community largely ineffective. Civilian democratic movement in exile with no clear path to power

96.name: New Zealand: Zero Carbon Act / Climate Governance Leadership
96.outcome_summary: New Zealand's Climate Change Response (Zero Carbon) Amendment Act (November 2019) is one of the most durable and technically credible climate laws globally. It passed 119-1 in parliament (unprecedented cross-party support), established an independent Climate Change Commission with legally binding advisory status, and set net-zero by 2050 for all gases except biogenic methane (reduction target by 2050). Subsequent National Party government (2023-) maintained core commitments despite campaigning on economic grounds. The act's institutional design — independent commission, legally binding emissions budgets, required government response to commission advice — is studied globally as a model of durable climate governance that can outlast changes in government.

progress_markers.event: Jacinda Ardern's Labour government wins election — climate change declared a 'nuclear-free moment for our generation.' Climate Change Commission concept included in Labour platform

Two-year consultation and stakeholder process — building genuine cross-party consensus rather than forcing through narrow majority. Business sector, Māori communities, agricultural sector all consulted

Climate Change Commission begins work — independent, technically credible, cross-spectrum membership. First emissions budgets published 2021
First NZ Emissions Budget (2022-2025) — government legally required to publish implementation plans in response to Commission advice

National Party wins election (October 2023) — more cautious on climate but does not repeal the Act. Core institutional framework maintained despite government change. Demonstrates institutional durability

97.name: Tanzania: Women's Land Rights
97.outcome_summary: Tanzania's women's land rights movement has been a 20+ year sustained campaign combining documentation, legal advocacy, and community organizing without a single tipping point. The Land Act (1999) formally recognized women's rights but customary law frequently overrode this in practice. HakiArdhi, LEAT, and international allies built a 'parallel legal system' of women's land rights documentation and clinics over two decades. Gains have been incremental: Land Tenure Support Program (LTSP) — joint government/donor initiative — registered millions of plots with women's names as co-owners (2014-2022). Pastoralist communities (Maasai women) remain particularly vulnerable. Score 2: incremental gains, no binding national transformation.

progress_markers.event: Tanzania Land Act passed — formally recognizes women's right to own land. But customary law in practice overrides statutory right in most rural areas

LTSP certificates issued in 80+ districts — millions of women receive co-ownership certificates for the first time. Significant but enforcement and divorce protections remain weak
LTSP coverage expanded. But Maasai evictions in Ngorongoro area (2022) for wildlife conservation expose continued vulnerability of pastoralist women's land security

Women's land rights in Tanzania: significant progress in formal registration, persistent gaps in customary inheritance, divorce, and community allocation. The 20-year campaign has produced real gains without a single national tipping point

98.name: Tajikistan/Central Asia: Water Rights and Climate Displacement
98.outcome_summary: The Aral Sea has shrunk 90% since 1960 — one of the 20th century's worst ecological disasters. Amu Darya and Syr Darya rivers remain heavily diverted for cotton agriculture. Climate change is accelerating Himalayan and Tian Shan glacial melt — providing short-term water surge then long-term shortage. Communities face acute water scarcity and agricultural collapse in Tajikistan, Uzbekistan, Kyrgyzstan, Kazakhstan. No political tipping has occurred because authoritarian governance in most countries prevents organized civil society response. No visual campaign of sufficient scale has made the catastrophe internationally legible. Score 1: awareness campaigns exist; no political tipping; crisis accelerating.

Soviet collapse leaves five new Central Asian states sharing the river systems with no functioning governance framework

Climate change begins accelerating glacial melt — Tian Shan glaciers retreating at 1% per year. Short-term water surge, long-term devastating shortage predicted
UN Special Programme for the Economies of Central Asia (SPECA) documents accelerating water stress. No regional political response
Aral Sea declared 'dead' for most practical purposes — the southern basin essentially gone. Kazakhstan's 'small Aral' restoration (Kok-Aral dam) partial success — northern section partially restored
Tajik-Kyrgyz water border conflicts escalate to armed clashes (April 2021, September 2022) — 50+ killed. Water scarcity emerging as conflict driver
Tajikistan glacier melt rate accelerating. Communities facing agriculture failure. Internal displacement beginning but not yet large-scale refugee movements

No political tipping. Authoritarian governance (Tajikistan, Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan) prevents organized civil society. International donors provide water efficiency aid without addressing governance root causes

99.name: Global: Digital Rights / Edward Snowden Revelations
99.outcome_summary: Snowden's June 2013 revelations about NSA/GCHQ mass surveillance produced: the USA FREEDOM Act (2015) reforming some NSA collection authorities; end-to-end encryption becoming mainstream (WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage adoption surged); GDPR (2018) partly influenced by post-Snowden privacy discourse; Tech companies' major encryption investments. However: mass surveillance systems largely continued; no criminal accountability for NSA officials; Snowden remains in Russia exile; subsequent US surveillance programs authorized under different authorities. Score 3: permanent cultural/institutional shift in encryption expectation; surveillance state itself largely unchanged.

100.name: Climate Justice: Vanuatu ICJ Advisory Opinion Campaign
100.outcome_summary: Vanuatu led a multi-year diplomatic campaign producing a UNGA resolution (April 2024, 132 co-sponsors) requesting an ICJ advisory opinion on states' climate obligations — the most states ever to participate in an ICJ proceeding (98 countries and 9 international organizations presented in September 2024 hearings). The advisory opinion is expected 2025-2026. While non-binding, an ICJ opinion on climate obligations would be the most authoritative legal statement ever made on the issue — providing the foundation for climate reparations, loss-and-damage financing, and future litigation globally. The diplomatic achievement itself (132-nation coalition built by a Pacific Island nation of 340,000) is already a landmark. Score 4: major institutional achievement, implementation pending opinion.

CARICOM, African Union, G77 bloc officially endorse ICJ advisory opinion request. Coalition crosses 120 states — sufficient for UNGA majority
April 29: UN General Assembly adopts resolution requesting ICJ advisory opinion — 132 co-sponsors. Largest co-sponsorship coalition in ICJ history. Pacific Island leadership of global majority

September 2024: ICJ public hearings at The Hague. 98 countries and 9 international organizations present oral arguments — largest ICJ proceeding in history. 'Pacific Warriors' youth activists featured
Hearings produce global legal record: state practice on climate obligations, loss and damage, intergenerational equity, rights of nature arguments all entered into international legal record

ICJ advisory opinion expected mid-2025 to early 2026. Climate litigation networks (Sabin Center, ClientEarth) have adaptation strategies ready for opinion. 132-nation coalition prepares accountability uses of the opinion
